- Yacht Clubs Are DYING – What REALLY Happened to Them: Kind: captions Language: en You ever walk past a yacht club lately and it just feels off? No, no energy, no kids sprinting down the dock and life jackets, just a quiet bar, a half empty deck, and the same three guys telling the same story about how it used to be. And it's weird because yacht clubs were supposed to be the heart of boating. They were the place you went when you wanted summer to feel like summer. Now, a lot of them feel like private retirement lounges with slips. So, today we're asking the real question. Are yacht clubs actually dying? And if they are, what really happened to them? Because here's the twist. It's not that people stopped loving the water. It's that the entire maritime world split in half and the middle got crushed. Stick to the end because I'm going to give you a simple yacht club survival checklist. If you're a member thinking about joining or you run a club, this checklist will tell you in 5 minutes whether your club is adapting or slowly sinking. And yes, subscribe if you want more videos like this because we're going to keep breaking down boating reality the way nobody else does. All right, let's rewind because the story of yacht clubs dying starts with the biggest illusion in boating, the co bump. For a minute, yacht clubs looked like they were saved. During 2019 to 2021, memberships exploded. Clubs saw insane growth, the kind of growth people hadn't seen in decades. And it made sense. Travel was dead. People were bored. Outdoor hobbies were the only thing that felt normal. So suddenly, everyone found boating again. People joined clubs. They bought boats. They signed their kids up for sailing programs. And for a brief moment, clubs thought, "We're back. But that wasn't a comeback. That was a temporary spike, a sugar rush. And once the world reopened, the crash came fast. Membership slid back down. Participation got weird. Retention got worse. And the clubs that were already struggling just went back to struggling. Only now with older docks, higher costs, and even less volunteer help. So when people say yacht clubs are dying, they're not noticing a slow decline. They're noticing a hard correction. And here's the deeper truth. Yacht clubs aren't dying evenly. They're bifurcating, splitting into two worlds. Because right now, boating is becoming a two-tier economy. At the top, the platinum tier, ultra exclusive clubs, prime real estate, mega yacht births, concier services, global reciprocity, full staff, and members who don't blink at a 5 figure initiation fee. That world isn't dying. That world is thriving. But the yacht club most people think of, the mid-tier, member-run, volunteer-driven club, that's the one taking water. And it's getting squeezed from both sides. It's getting squeezed by the wealthy and by the access economy. And we need to talk about the access economy because that's the real disruptor. boat clubs, sharing fleets, community sailing centers, memberships where you don't own the boat. You just book it, show up, and go. It's the Netflix version of boating, and it's exploding because modern people don't want the ownership burden. They want the experience. So, the old yacht club is stuck in the middle. It's not luxury enough to compete with the platinum clubs, and it's not simple enough to compete with shared access. That's the death zone. and the clubs living in that zone are starting to feel empty. Now, let's talk about why the mid-tier model is collapsing. Because it comes down to one thing, the volunteer model broke. Yacht clubs were built on sweat equity. That Corinthian ideal. The whole club runs because members do the work. People paint the clubhouse, run races, repair docks, serve on committees, handle finances. That model worked when society had time. Back then, a lot of households had one income. Weekends were open. People had margin in their lives. And you could actually show up and do work hours without feeling like you were sacrificing your entire month. But that world is gone. Now most households are dual income. People work longer hours. Kids are in year round sports. Weekends are booked solid. And the very thing yacht clubs depended on, free labor, evaporated. So what happens? The club has to hire staff. Docmaster, general manager, maintenance crew, bar staff, event coordinator. And the moment you hire staff, your fixed costs spike. Payroll taxes, benefits, insurance, compliance. Suddenly, the club has overhead like a business, but it's still operating like a volunteer nonprofit. That's the professionalization trap. And it creates a vicious cycle. You can literally watch it happen. volunteer deficit, then paid staff, then dues go up, then initiation fees go up, then young members get priced out, then membership gets older, then volunteer deficit gets worse, then you need more staff, then dues go up again, and now the club is too expensive for entry-level sailors, but still not luxurious enough to justify the price. So, it becomes a bad deal for the exact demographic it needs to survive. Now, we need to talk about money because even if the culture was perfect, the math is getting brutal. Boating in general got expensive, but yacht clubs got expensive in a specific way. Because the yacht club lifestyle stacks fees on top of fees, you already pay slip, insurance, maintenance, storage, fuel, repairs, and now you add club dues, initiation fee, dining minimum, work hours, event fees, and sometimes you don't even get a slip. You're paying to be part of the club while your boat sits somewhere else. So for a young family, it's not just can we afford boating, it's can we afford boating plus the club package. And for a lot of people, the answer is no. Not because they hate yacht clubs because it just doesn't pencil out. And here's the thing yacht clubs don't want to admit. Modern consumers do not pay for tradition. They pay for value. And value is measured in convenience, experience, and flexibility. Which brings us to the next killer, culture. Because even when a club isn't that expensive, it can still feel unwelcoming. Say yacht club out loud. Most people picture the stereotype instantly. Blazers, captain's hats, old rules, old money vibes, a membership process that feels like a background check. And yes, a lot of clubs have moved away from that. But the reputation stuck. And modern generations hate that vibe. They want casual, inclusive, fun. No gatekeeping, no weird hierarchy. No, you can't sit there. If your club still feels like a 1950s country club, you are not pulling in the next generation. Now add in something bigger, something that's quietly destroying clubs from the inside. Insurance. Because it's not just owners struggling to ensure their boats. It's clubs struggling to ensure their waterfront infrastructure, docks, buildings, liability, named storm risk. And in places like Florida, insurance isn't just expensive, it's unstable. Premiums jump, coverage shrinks, deductibles become insane. Some policies exclude windstorm damage for overwater structures, which is basically like saying, "We'll insure you unless the ocean does ocean things." So, clubs are forced into a trap. Either raise dues to build huge reserves or operate exposed. And for a mid-tier nonprofit club, one bad storm without proper coverage can end the institution. That's not drama. That's reality. And climate risk makes this worse every year. Sunny day flooding, tide intrusion, corrosion in electrical systems, higher storm surge, docks that were built for the sea level of the 1970s are now being tested by the sea level of today. And updating that infrastructure, it's not a couple volunteers with a wrench. It's a capital project. engineers, permits, environmental compliance, bulk ad upgrades, floating docks, power upgrades, and the bill is huge. Which leads to the final structural problem. The boats changed. And a lot of older clubs didn't. Modern boats are wider. Catamarans are everywhere. Yachts are bigger. People want power. They want beam. They want comfort. But a lot of older clubs have slips designed for 30foot sloops from 1978. narrow, shallow, low power, not built for wide cats or high demand electrical loads. So, the club ends up with a weird mismatch. Demand for slips is high, but not for those slips. People with expensive boats bypass the club. They go to modern marinas or platinum clubs or private facilities, and the mid-tier club loses relevance. So now you've got this perfect storm. Volunteer model collapse, professionalization trap, cost barrier, cultural mismatch, insurance pressure, infrastructure obsolescence, boat evolution, and then the access economy shows up and says, "Hey, want the water without the headache?" And people take that deal. That's why boat clubs are winning because they delete the three things that make ownership miserable. cleaning, cost surprises, capital tied up. You book, you show up, you go, you leave, and someone else deals with maintenance. That model is insanely attractive to millennials and Gen Z. And it's not because they don't like commitment. It's because they're used to paying for access. They subscribe to everything. Why would boating be different? So, yacht clubs are losing the middle. Not because boating is dead, but because the old format is outdated. But here's the part people miss. Yacht clubs can absolutely survive. Some are thriving right now. And the ones thriving all have one thing in common. They stopped acting like a museum. They modernized the value proposition. So what does that look like? It looks like social marina energy, not membersonly dining room energy. It looks like casual waterfront fun, taco nights, dock parties, live music, oprey sale vibes, shorts and flipflops accepted. Families feel welcome. You want people to show up even if they don't race. Because here's another mistake clubs make. They build everything around racing. But racing is niche. Most boers don't want to race. They want sunset cruises, paddle boarding, kaying, fishing off the dock, movie nights, community events. So, the clubs surviving are diversifying. They are becoming water clubs, not just sailing clubs, and they're fixing the biggest barrier of all, boat ownership. Because the smartest clubs are building clubowned fleets, small keelboats, day sailors, maybe even a few center consoles, boats members can use without owning. It's the internal version of the boat club model. And it works because now a 28-year-old can join, learn, crew, sail, meet people, get hooked without needing to buy a 40-year-old fixerupper just to participate. That's how you build a pipeline. That's how you survive. And the final piece, visibility. Some yacht clubs are invisible online. Websites from 2003. No social media, no easy signups, no schedules, no photos, nothing that makes a younger person think that looks fun. The clubs that thrive, they show up. Instagram, YouTube, Tik Tok, they post the sunset photos, the kids sailing, the dock parties, the casual vibe. They make the club feel alive. Because if your club doesn't show up in someone's feed, it doesn't exist. Now, like I promised, here's your yacht club survival checklist. If you're watching this and you're wondering whether your club is adapting or slowly sinking, listen to this. A club that survives in 2026 does these things. Number one, it offers memberships that don't require boat ownership. Social members, dining members, crew memberships. Number two, it has a clubowned fleet or a real shared boat program. Number three, it feels casual and welcoming, less formality, more fun. Number four, it offers activities that aren't racing, families, paddle sports, community events. Number five, it is modern online, simple website, easy signups, active social presence. And number six, it has an infrastructure plan. Not hope, not we'll deal with it later, a plan, insurance strategy, reserves, dock upgrades, adaptation. If a club doesn't have those things, it's going to struggle. Not because it's a bad club, because the world changed. And that's the real conclusion. Yacht clubs aren't dying because people stopped loving the water. They're dying because they stopped keeping up with the world around them. The sea didn't change, society did. So, the yacht club isn't dead, it's shedding its skin. The middle model is collapsing while luxury clubs thrive and access-based boating explodes. The yacht club is dying. Long live the yacht club. If you enjoyed this, hit like, subscribe for more boating reality, and send this to someone who still thinks yacht clubs are just fine. - Your Boat Broker Doesn't Want You Seeing These Numbers: Kind: captions Language: en A mechanic doing the work on your boat earns $26.42 an hour. You get billed 150 to 180 dollars. That gap is not just overhead, it's a signal. Before you ever meet that mechanic, three other parties have already locked in their cut. The broker who sold you the boat, the charter company promising to offset your costs, and the marina charging you to park it. Each one collects before you see a dollar. The question this video answers is, was that an accident? Annual operating costs for a privately owned boat fall into a narrow, predictable band. The National Marine Manufacturers Association pegs the total at 20 to 25% of the original purchase price each year. This figure absorbs everything. Fuel, insurance, slip fees, [music] scheduled maintenance, haul-outs, and the steady parade of repairs. For a $600,000 boat, that means 120,000 to 150,000 dollars leave the owner's account every 12 months, whether the boat moves or not. Financing pushes the drain higher. With a standard 20% down payment and a 5-year loan, the cash outflow rises to 27 to 30% of the purchase price per year. Interest alone widens the gap between what the owner pays and what the boat is worth at any given moment. Depreciation, meanwhile, works quietly in the background. The National Marine Manufacturers Association resale tables show that a typical boat loses 25 to 30% of its value in the first 5 years. A $600,000 boat is worth 420,000 to 450,000 dollars after half a decade, before factoring in market swings or cosmetic neglect. These three numbers, 20 to 25% for operating, 27 to 30% for financed ownership, and a 25 to 30% drop in value, form the baseline ledger, long before any promises of charter income or tax advantages enter the conversation. The promise of boat ownership always rests on the idea of use, sir. Sunlit days on the water, spontaneous weekends away. In reality, the average US owner takes their boat out just 29 days a year, according to the National Marine Manufacturers Association's most recent survey. That is less than 1 day in 12. Every dollar spent on insurance, slip fees, and scheduled maintenance stretches across months of inactivity, compounding the true cost of each outing. Service and repairs introduce a second, quieter drain. The Bureau of Labor Statistics lists the average marine mechanic's wage at $26.42 per hour. Yet, when an owner signs a repair order at the marina service shop, the bill is between $150 and $180 per hour, or four to seven times the technician's take-home pay. The difference covers shop overhead, parts markups, and a profit margin that, in practice, is locked in regardless of the outcome for the owner. Each underused day amplifies the impact of these markups, turning minor issues into expensive episodes. The numbers invite a simple question. If the owner pays full freight for every hour and every idle slip, who actually benefits from the spread? The answer begins to surface in the referral networks and contract structures that shape every transaction from sale to service. At the closing table, the arithmetic is silent, but absolute. On a million-dollar boat, the commission agreement, standard in nearly every broker contract, calls for 10% of the sale price. That is $100,000 due in full the moment the deal funds. The seller pays this sum, but the buyer's broker collects half, $50,000, by right of a co-brokering clause embedded in the paperwork. The listing broker receives the other half, regardless of whether the buyer found the boat through an internet search or a personal referral. This split is not a handshake or a backroom arrangement. It is written into every major brokerage agreement, confirmed by the National Marine Manufacturers Association, and spelled out in the published policies of the International Yacht Brokers Association. The commission is calculated on the gross sale price before any deductions for repairs or after-sale adjustments. Once the wire transfer clears, the brokers' shares are released, often within days, long before the new owner has even received a bill of sale or set foot on their vessel as its legal steward. No matter how the negotiation unfolded, or how hard the buyer pressed for a lower price, the commission remains fixed. There is no sliding scale for effort, no adjustment for market conditions, or the complexity of the transaction. The 10% rule applies to a $60,000 and a $6 million motor yacht alike. Only at the very highest end, above $10 million, do some brokerages invoke a sliding tier, but even then, the first $10 million is charged at full freight. The commission structure creates a moment of pure alignment for the brokers and a permanent asymmetry for the buyer. The buyer's broker is paid by the seller, not the client whose interests they nominally represent. The seller's broker, meanwhile, is incentivized to close at the highest possible price, knowing every percentage point is more money in the payout. The entire sum is due at closing, immune to the future fortunes of the boat, the buyer's experience, or the eventual resale value. The money flows up front, locked in by contract, and the brokers exit the scene richer by tens of thousands before a single cost of ownership is counted. A code of ethics published by the American Boat and Yacht Council governs how brokers may interact with surveyors. The rule is clear. Brokers must disclose any financial relationship with a surveyor and may not accept more than $250 in referral fees. The buyer must receive a list of at least three independent surveyors, and the broker's compensation cannot depend on which one is chosen. The intent is to shield the inspection process from hidden influence, but in practice, the referral network remains a source of quiet leverage. The inspection, on which the entire sale depends, is never truly neutral when the broker controls the list. Court records show how this tension moves from legal code to the docket. In February 2024, Yarmond Expeditions filed suit in Delaware District Court, naming the International Yacht Brokers Association as defendant. The complaint alleged that member brokers charged excessive commissions and failed to enforce the ABYC's disclosure rules. The suit was dismissed in January 2025 by Judge K. Michael Moore, who found that the dispute fell within the boundaries of private contract and that the plaintiff lacked standing. The commission structure and the referral ecosystem remained intact. A second case, Brill Maritime versus Boats Group, filed in August 2025 in New York, attacks the digital backbone of the brokerage network itself. Brill Maritime claims that Boats Group, which owns YachtWorld, BoatTrader, and boats.com, controls roughly 75% of all online boat listings in the United States. The complaint details a subscription-based model. Brokers must pay annual fees to list vessels, and any listing is locked in for 12 months. Early removal triggers a penalty equal to half the remaining contract value. The lock-in clause, quoted directly in the court filing, prevents brokers from moving listings freely between platforms and cements Boats Group's near monopoly over the digital marketplace. As of May 2026, the case remains pending. The pattern is not hidden. Brokers are paid before the buyer takes possession. Referral fees, capped but persistent, flow through networks that serve the transaction first and the owner second. The listing platforms, which brokers must pay to access, enforce their own rules through binding contracts. Each layer is individually rational, defensible even, but together, they create a system where the owner's upside is last in line, and legal challenges have so far only confirmed the structure. Charter management contracts spell out the arithmetic long before any owner sees a dollar. The standard agreement, used by most United States charter firms, and echoed in every public-facing contract, is built around a simple sequence. Gross charter revenue comes in. A management fee of 15 to 20% is deducted off the top, and only then are the remaining operating costs, fuel, crew, dockage, and provisioning, subtracted to reveal what is left for the owner. The fee is not negotiable, not performance-based, and not subject to refund if bookings fall short. It is owed on every dollar of revenue, whether the season is a sellout or a washout. The industry pitch leans on occupancy math, promising that a well-placed yacht can offset its costs or even turn a profit if it reaches a certain number of charter weeks per year. The break-even threshold, buried in management brochures and contract footnotes, sits at 16 to 20 weeks of paid bookings. This figure is not a marketing flourish. It is the dividing line between a vessel that covers its own slip fees and one that quietly bleeds cash. For a 60-foot motor yacht, the difference between 12 weeks booked and 18 is the difference between a manageable annual loss and a five-figure shortfall. The management fee is always calculated on gross revenue, not net. If a boat earns $100,000 in charter bookings, the operator takes $15,000 to $20,000 before a single expense is paid. Fuel, cleaning, and repairs are then deducted from what remains, often consuming another 30% or more. The owner receives only the residue with no recourse for seasons cut short by weather, mechanical issues, or market downturns. Contracts reviewed from Europe, Yacht Charter, INT Marine, and Catamaran Guru all confirm this sequence. Managers cut first, operating costs next, owner's share last. This structure produces a quiet but absolute mismatch between the promise and the likely outcome. Most owners, even those who sign up with the intent to maximize charter weeks, struggle to reach the 16 to 20-week break-even point. Industry utilization studies show that actual occupancy often falls short, especially outside peak season or in markets with heavy competition. For the majority, the management fee is not a path to upside but a fixed toll collected regardless of the owner's net result. The arithmetic is not a secret, but it is rarely emphasized at the point of sale. Instead, it sits in the middle of the contract, quietly shifting risk away from the manager and onto the person who signed the check. Owner B's ledger for the first full charter season reads like a case study in subtraction. The year began with promise. A recently purchased $850,000 catamaran placed with a national charter operator and a calendar open for bookings. The gross charter revenue at the end of the season came in at $62,000. That is the top line, the number featured in every sales pitch and owner update. But the arithmetic starts before the first dollar ever reaches the owner's account. The charter company deducted a management fee of $12,400, exactly 20% of gross, matching the industry standard. This fee is automatic, applied to every booking, and not contingent on how many weeks the boat is actually chartered or whether the bookings cover the owner's costs. The contract is clear. The manager's cut comes off the top every time. Next came the operating expenses, fuel, crew, dockage, and provisioning. These totaled $18,600, about 30% of gross. Each line item is supported by invoices and receipts, but the owner has no practical control over the final tally. The operator selects the vendors, books the crew, and arranges the services, then sends the bill. After these deductions, the net before maintenance stands at $31,000. But the season's real impact appears in the repair and maintenance file. Charter-driven use accelerated engine hours, wore down upholstery, and pushed routine service to twice the frequency of private ownership. The total maintenance and repair outlay for the year was $28,000. This figure includes actual repairs, such as engine work, hull touch-ups, and electronics troubleshooting, as well as the extra service costs triggered by the high-volume charter calendar. Every invoice is date-stamped and itemized, from the $12,000 engine overhaul to the $8,000 hull gelcoat repair. When the dust settles, Owner B's cash flow for the entire season is $3,000. That is the residue left after $62,000 in bookings, $12,400 in management fees, $18,600 in operating expenses, and $28,000 in repairs. The promised upside never materializes. The numbers do not hide or soften. The owner's net is $3,000, less than the cost of a single week's charter for a guest. The ledger is not a warning, it is a record. Each deduction is spelled out in the contract, each invoice is real, and the arithmetic is inescapable. Slip fees do not stand still. In June 2024, Dana Point Harbor notified berth holders of increases ranging from 4.71% to 15.46% depending on slip size and location. The notice was spare, adjustments to reflect market conditions and infrastructure investments. For many, the new rates arrived without negotiation, and the contract's escalation clause made protest irrelevant. Owners who had signed multi-year leases found themselves bound by language that permitted annual increases at the harbor's discretion, subject only to 30 days written notice. San Francisco's municipal marina pushed even harder. In 2024, the city proposed a 31.4% jump for premium slips, citing deferred maintenance and rising operating costs. The public comment period yielded pages of objections, but the escalation clause in the standard slip agreement left little room for maneuver. The clause reads, "Marina reserves the right to adjust slip rental fees [music] annually. Notice of increase shall be provided not less than 30 days in advance. No cap, no negotiation, no exit without penalty." These are not isolated cases. A [music] 2024 industry survey found that 84% of US marinas raised rates in the previous 70% increased slip fees, and 60% raised ancillary charges, pump-out, electricity, parking, often by more than the consumer price index. The escalation is not a function of supply and demand alone. It is written into the structure of the lease, reinforced by the owner's inability to move a 30,000-lb vessel on short notice. The power does not rest with a single manager or a faceless corporation, but with municipal boards and harbor authorities who set rates by vote or fiat. Their decisions are buffered by contract clauses that shield them from market pushback. The owner's only recourse is to accept the new rate or begin the costly process of relocating. A process that itself is often blocked by wait lists, transfer fees, and regional scarcity. The result is a form of local captivity, engineered less by conspiracy than by the slow, silent machinery of public contract and administrative discretion. Safe Harbor Marinas changed the scale of the game. On February 24th, 2025, Blackstone Infrastructure closed a $5.65 billion deal to acquire Safe Harbor, then the largest marina owner in the United States. The transaction valued the business at 21 times its annual earnings, a multiple more common in tech than in boat slips. The logic was simple. Marina contracts, with their annual escalation clauses and owner lock-in, offered the kind of predictable, inflation-resistant cash flow that private equity covets. For boat owners, the effect was invisible but absolute. [music] The slip became less a place to keep a boat and more a revenue stream to [music] be packaged, traded, and leveraged. In the months that followed, Safe Harbor did not pause to digest. In November 2025, it added Port Annapolis, a 250-slip facility on the Chesapeake Bay, to its growing stable. Each acquisition extended the network, and each new contract imported the same escalation language, annual increases at management's discretion, 30 days notice, and no practical avenue for protest or exit. Owners who had once negotiated with local dockmasters now faced institutional landlords backed by Wall Street, whose primary obligation was to investors, not tenants. The scale of consolidation is not theoretical. After the acquisition, Safe Harbor's portfolio spanned more than 140 locations nationwide. The business model depends on the owner's inability to move their vessel easily or to find alternative moorage without wait lists and transfer fees. The marina, once a local service, is now a financial asset class. The fee increases, the lock-in periods, and the escalation clauses are not isolated tactics. They are the foundation of a system that treats every slip as a captive annuity. For the individual owner, the terms are spelled out in the lease, but the power to contest them has shifted far beyond the harbor office. The economics are not accidental. They are engineered contract by contract across a network now valued in the billions. Today, the mechanics of ownership remain unchanged. Each intermediary's contract is structured to extract value before you see a return. Private equity now owns more than 25% of US marina slips, and brokerage incentives remain aligned against owner upside. The arithmetic is public, but the consequences are personal. Clarity is not a guarantee of profit, but it is the only defense left. What you sign determines who wins. Let me know your perspective below.

So, bonus depreciation's been around since 2002 and it went up, it went down, it's been all over the place. But more recently, um in 2022, it went to 100% bonus depreciation. uh 2023 well Congress decided it would go to 100 and then they would reduce by 20% per year until it was absolutely and until it was back to zero. So 2022 it was 100%. 2023 went to 80% 2024 went to 60% 2025 this year 40% and next year would have been 20 and the following year it would have been phased out but it's back. Um so what Congress has decided in the new bill that was passed is that uh bonus depreciation be moved back to 100%. And it will be retroactive till the 1st of January 2025. So 2025 is the first year and 100% bonus depreciation I believe will be steady until 2030. - Why Brand New Sailboats Are a Terrible Investment Right Now: Kind: captions Language: en Why would anyone buy a brand new sailboat in 2026? Seriously, why would anyone walk into a boat show, sit down with a dealer, sign a contract at full manufacturer's suggested retail price, and take delivery of a vessel that will lose more value in the first 3 years than most people spend on a car? Because the numbers right now are brutal. And they're not just bad in the way that boats have always been expensive, they're bad in a new way, a structural way, a way that makes the traditional new boat premium look almost quaint compared to what's actually happening to owners in this market. Because in 2026, you're not just paying for a new boat, you're paying for inflated material costs that the manufacturers have passed directly to you. You're paying for electronics that will be outdated before your warranty expires. You're paying interest rates that are double what they were 4 years ago. You're paying into a regulatory environment that has decided private yacht ownership is a convenient target for tax revenue. And you're doing all of this while the used market is flooding with nearly identical boats 3 to 5 years old, fully equipped, already depreciated, and sitting unsold. That is the 2026 new sailboat trap, and it is worse than most buyers realize. Let's start with the thing everyone knows but almost nobody quantifies properly, depreciation. The moment you take delivery of a new sailboat, you lose money. That's always been true, but the speed and depth of that loss in 2026 has changed. In a balanced market like this one, the initial new boat penalty sits at around 10 to 20% within the first year alone. That's not wear and tear, that's not damage, that's just the market adjusting for the fact that the boat is no longer new. By year 3, you've typically lost 30 to 40% of the original purchase price. By year 5, you're looking at a cumulative loss of around 50%. So, on a $400,000 boat, you could be sitting on $200,000 of evaporated equity in 5 years, and you haven't crashed it, you haven't neglected it, you just owned it. That's the baseline, but here's where 2026 makes it worse. The depreciation curve has steepened because of something the manufacturers are doing to themselves. They're launching new models at a pace that makes their own recent inventory look old. The Beneteau Jeanneau Group alone introduced 66 new models over a 3-year period. 66. That is an absurd rate of product iteration, and every single new model launch effectively ages every existing model in the lineup. A boat that is only 10 to 15 years old now appears significantly more dated than a vessel of the same age would have looked a decade ago. Because the styling changes faster, the cockpit layouts evolve faster, the helm technology moves faster, and buyers on the secondary market can see it. They can compare, they can scroll through listings and immediately tell which generation a boat belongs to. That visual aging effect hammers resale velocity. It makes late model boats harder to sell, and it makes new boats depreciate faster because the next refresh is never far away. So, you're not just losing value because boats lose value, you're losing value because the manufacturer is actively designing your boat into obsolescence. That is accelerated depreciation driven by the people who sold you the boat in the first place. And it gets worse when you zoom out from individual brands and look at what's happening to inventory globally because the used market right now is flooded. The pandemic created a buying frenzy between 2021 and 2024. People who had never owned boats suddenly wanted boats. People who had small boats suddenly wanted bigger boats. Money was cheap, fear of missing out was real, and the industry responded by ramping production and raising prices. Now, those pandemic era buyers are exiting. They're selling, and they're selling into a market that doesn't have enough demand to absorb what they're listing. In the Caribbean alone, there are 387 catamarans for sale. 73% of them are under 10 years old, and the turnover rate on those listings is 24%. That means three out of four listed boats are not finding buyers. That is a completely abnormal supply-demand imbalance, and it creates a ceiling on the resale value of any new boat purchased today. Because why would a rational buyer pay full price for a new 45-footer when they can find a 2022 model, already equipped, already sea-trialed, already passed its worst depreciation, sitting on a dock somewhere in the Caribbean with an increasingly motivated seller? That is the competitive environment a new boat enters the moment it leaves the factory, and it is devastating. Now, layer on top of that what's happening with manufacturing costs because here's the part that feels almost unfair. The price of a new boat has gone up not because the boat is dramatically better, but because the raw materials that go into building it have gotten more expensive, and the manufacturers have passed every cent of that increase to the buyer. Fiberglass. The global fiberglass market is valued at over $21 billion in 2025, growing at nearly 6% annually. Marine manufacturers are competing for materials, especially E-glass, which remains the industry standard for hull construction. But the price of silica, resins, and petroleum-based additives has been volatile. Rising oil prices, driven by geopolitical instability, have a cascading effect on resin costs and shipping. That has compressed manufacturer margins by 3 to 5% since 2023, and instead of absorbing that compression, the builders have raised retail prices. Then, there's electronics. Modern sailboats now ship with integrated digital navigation, automated sail handling, digital monitoring systems, and connectivity suites as standard equipment. That sounds impressive until you realize what it means for pricing and for resale because the copper-clad laminates that form the base of most marine circuit boards have seen price adjustments of up to 30% in 2026. That cost gets baked into the helm suite, the power management system, and the switching panels. And here's the cruel part, those electronics age faster than the hull. A fiberglass hull from 2026 will still be structurally sound in 2040. The navigation system will be a museum piece. So, you're paying a premium for technology that depreciates at the speed of consumer electronics bolted onto a hull that depreciates at the speed of a boat, the worst of both worlds. And when you try to sell that boat in 5 years, the buyer is going to look at the electronics and see something dated. They won't care that the chart plotter cost $4,000 when it was new, they'll care that it doesn't support the latest software, doesn't integrate with their phone properly, and looks like it belongs in a different era. That is dead weight on your resale value, technology that cost you real money on the way in and gives you nothing on the way out. Now, let's talk about what it actually costs to finance a new sailboat in 2026 because this is where the math gets truly painful. The era of cheap money is over. Interest rates for marine loans have climbed to levels that fundamentally change the economics of ownership. For a buyer with excellent credit, you might see rates starting around 6% for someone with good credit, which means a score between 670 and 739, the average boat loan rate has risen to nearly 9%. Let's make that real. Say you're buying a $150,000 sailboat, you put down 30,000, you finance 120,000 over 10 years at around 8 and 1/2% your monthly payment is roughly $1,300. Over the life of the loan, you will pay over $60,000 in interest alone. $60,000. That is not building equity, that is not going toward the boat. That is pure cost of borrowing, and while you're paying that interest, the boat is depreciating at 8 to 10% per year. So, you are simultaneously losing value on the asset and paying a premium for the privilege of losing that value. That is the financial structure of new boat ownership in 2026, and it is objectively terrible. For higher value purchases, the numbers scale proportionally. A $250,000 loan at 6 and 1/2% over 10 years means monthly payments around $2,700. A million-dollar yacht loan, even at the best available rate, means monthly payments above 10,000. Some ultra-high net worth buyers try to work around this with Lombard loans, borrowing against investment portfolios instead of using traditional marine financing. But even those facilities had become more conservative in 2026. Banks are evaluating not just the borrower's credit, but the vessel's manufacturer reputation and potential resale liquidity because the banks have done the math, too. They know what happens to boat values, and they're protecting themselves accordingly. Now, if all of that weren't enough, there is a new layer of cost that didn't exist 5 years ago, regulation. Governments, particularly in Europe, have decided that private yacht ownership is an easy political target, and the fiscal consequences for owners are getting heavier every year. France has led the charge. The French Finance Act of 2026 introduced a 20% tax on what they call non-professional assets held by holding companies. That specifically targets yachts and private jets. The idea is to close the loophole where wealthy individuals categorized personal boats as business equipment to avoid wealth taxes. The tax applies to the fair market value of the vessel, 20% on top of that. France maintains a surcharge on private yachts over 30 m, ranging from 30,000 to 200,000 euros. The National Assembly voted to keep this in the 2026 budget, which signals a permanent political appetite for yacht-specific taxation. This is not a one-time adjustment. This is a policy direction, and other European governments are watching. Then, there's the environmental side. The European Union's Fuel EU Maritime Regulation now requires a 2% improvement in greenhouse gas intensity against a 2020 baseline. The EU Emissions Trading System has expanded beyond carbon dioxide to include methane and nitrous oxide. That shift is particularly punitive for vessels using liquefied natural gas or dual fuel systems because of methane slip, the escape of unburned methane during combustion. For owners of large, modern cruising sailboats that use these advanced propulsion systems to meet green standards, compliance just got significantly more expensive. And a 2026 review is already scheduled to determine whether these requirements should be extended to smaller vessels. So, the regulatory direction is clear. More taxes, more emissions rules, more compliance costs, more friction. And all of it lands on the owner. Now, let's talk about what it actually costs to keep a new sailboat once you own it because the purchase price is not the real price. The real price is the ongoing bleed. The industry has a rough guideline called the 10% rule. It suggests that annual maintenance and operations will cost roughly 10% of the vessel's purchase price. So, a $300,000 boat would cost around 30,000 a year just to maintain and operate. And in 2026, that rule is often an underestimate for new, technically complex vessels. For boats in the zero to five-year age range, the maintenance factor runs between 1,200 and $1,800 per foot per year. That includes professional servicing required to maintain warranties, specialized electronics maintenance, and the detailing premium expected for a vessel that's supposed to look new. On a 45-ft boat, that translates to somewhere between 54,000 and 80,000 dollars a year in maintenance costs alone. Then, add docking fees. In South Florida, a major boating market, marina rates in 2026 run between 18 and 40 dollars per foot per month. Premium marinas charge 45 dollars or more per foot. For a 40-ft yacht, that is an annual docking cost of 8,600 to 19,000 dollars. In the Mediterranean, it's worse. Daily birthing rates for a 40-ft yacht at premium locations range from 200 to 400 euros during summer months. A 60-footer can expect 400 to 800 euros per day. And those prices often exclude utility surcharges and luxury taxes, which can add 20 to 40% on top. Then, there's insurance. Typically, 1 and 1/2 to 3% of hull value annually. On a $400,000 boat, that's 6 to 12,000 a year. Add it all up, monthly payments on the loan, insurance, docking, maintenance, depreciation, the true monthly cost of owning a new 30-ft sailboat in 2026 often exceeds $2,500. For a 45-footer, you're looking at multiples of that. And every single month, the underlying asset is worth less than it was the month before. That is the financial reality, not the brochure reality, not the boat show reality, the spreadsheet reality. And the spreadsheet does not lie. Here's the part that makes all of this feel almost absurd. The technology that makes a new boat feel cutting-edge today is the same technology that will make it feel dated tomorrow. Manufacturers in 2026 are heavily marketing smart, electric, and hybrid propulsion systems. Fontaine Pajot has set a target for 50% of its models to be available with electric or hybrid options. And these systems genuinely work. They're quieter, they're cleaner, they align with the environmental regulations, they attract the green luxury buyer. But, they present an enormous long-term risk to the vessel's value because unlike a traditional diesel engine, which has a service life measured in decades and a well-established secondary market, marine electric propulsion is in a state of rapid evolution. A buyer acquiring an electric sailboat in 2026 is investing in version 1.0 technology. By 2030, advances in solid-state batteries or hydrogen fuel cells may render current lithium-ion systems technologically obsolete and commercially worthless for resale. And the lack of shoreside charging infrastructure in many marinas creates a practical constraint that limits the vessel's cruising range and desirability right now. So, you're paying a massive premium for propulsion technology that may have a shorter useful commercial life than the hull it's installed in. That is the definition of a bad investment. And it's not just electric systems. The integration of AI-driven navigation and satellite connectivity has become a standard expectation. Late-model used boats from 2022 to 2024 are already entering the market with these upgrades pre-installed. A new buyer in 2026 pays full retail for these features. A used buyer acquires them as part of the depreciated hull value. That gap, the difference in cost per feature between new and used, has never been wider than it is right now. So, what is actually happening in the market? The big manufacturers are recovering. Group Beneteau, the world's largest sailboat manufacturer, saw a 26.6% contraction in its sailing business in 2025. But, the company confirmed a return to growth in 2026, driven by a 24% increase in order intake. Hanse Yachts reported a revenue turnaround to 105 million euros in late 2025, with production running at full capacity by early 2026. Fontaine Pajot landed triple boat of the year nominations for 2026. This sounds like good news, but for the buyer, it's actually the opposite because the surge in production capacity is aimed at clearing backlogs that extend into mid-2026 for certain high-demand models. That means a buyer ordering today is locking in 2026 peak pricing for a vessel they may not receive until the market has further softened. You are buying at the top. And by the time you take delivery, the secondary market may have moved further against you. The manufacturers are optimizing for their shareholders, not for your equity. And there's one more structural shift that matters. Consumer behavior is changing. The access over ownership model is gaining traction. Boat clubs and fractional ownership programs are growing because they solve the math problem that private ownership cannot. In the United States, memberships starting from around $600 a month now include captain, fuel, and docking. Those are costs that would exceed $4,000 monthly for a private owner. When a boat club can offer the experience of boating at a fraction of the cost, with none of the depreciation, none of the maintenance, none of the insurance, and none of the docking fees, the pool of potential buyers for your five-year-old boat in 2031 is going to be smaller and more price-sensitive than it is today. That is the demand side of the equation collapsing. Fewer traditional buyers, more alternatives, more price sensitivity, more leverage for the buyer, less for the seller. And if you're the seller, the seller is you five years from now, trying to get out of a boat you bought new at peak pricing. So, what's the rational path? The answer is almost painfully obvious, the late-model brokerage market. Buying a three-to-five-year-old vessel in 2026 allows you to acquire an asset where the steepest portion of the depreciation curve has already been absorbed by the first owner. These boats are typically still under warranty or recently out of it. They carry modern electronics, modern sail handling, and modern safety equipment. They've been sea-trialed. Their systems have been tested. Their weak points have been identified and often corrected. And they're available in a market where sellers are facing historically low turnover rates, which means negotiating leverage is on your side. You get more boat for less money. You avoid the worst of the depreciation hit. You side-step the new boat premium on materials and electronics. And you enter a market where every month that passes gives you slightly more buying power because the inventory keeps growing, and the sellers keep getting more motivated. That is not speculation. That is structural. So, no. Buying a brand new sailboat in 2026 is not just expensive, it is irrational. Unless you have a specific reason that justifies the premium, a custom build, a particular configuration that doesn't exist on the secondary market, a business use case with tax advantages, there is almost no financial scenario where new acquisition outperforms a disciplined used purchase. The depreciation is steeper than it's been in a decade. The financing is the most expensive in 20 years. The regulatory burden is accelerating. The maintenance costs are at record highs. The technology cycle is punishing early adopters. The used market is oversupplied with recent well-equipped inventory. And the cultural shift toward access over ownership is quietly eroding the future buyer pool. That is the 2026 new sailboat market. Not a luxury purchase, a depreciating liability caught between rising costs, falling resale values, and a secondary market that offers almost everything the new market does for dramatically less money. The only question is whether buyers will keep paying the premium for the smell of a new boat or whether they'll finally do the math.

keep your boat looking like new and hold its value better one wash your boat with ample water and soap never dry wash your boat do it multiple times a year and wax your boat at least once at the beginning the season to keep it sealed and keep the sun damage at a minimum to store your boat indoors as much as possible if available put your boat in winter storage indoors and if possible keep your boat in a boathouse or undercover in the summer to limit the UV damage 3 never let your company step on your vinyl boat seats that extra pressure really puts a lot of tension on the seams and can pull it apart also don't ever wash your vinyl with a harsh chemical soap and water is fine or I'm like a McGuire's vinyl restorer but never something harsh thank you for joining us on let's go of lessons of boating for today's video remember to like this video and subscribe for more anything have fun and we'll see you out on the water

However..if you have a boat that's greater than 8½ foot beam, or it's too big for you to tow with whatever vehicle you own, you have to keep your boat at a marina, and they usually force you to use them to winterize, shrink wrap, summerize, and store...on top of monthly dock fees. So except for the first situation, owning a boat IS EXPENSIVE.
I owned a 1987 Ski Centurion competition ski boat for 8 years and sold it for $600 less than I bought it for. My next boat was a 1994 Wellcraft Nova Spyder. I bought it for $17K from the original owner who had paid $65K for the boat. He paid almost 4X what I paid. Never ever buy a new  boat unless you love wasting money.

- Is It Better To Buy A New Or Used Boat Regarding Depreciation? - Boat Life Masters: Kind: captions Language: en Is it better to buy a new or used boat regarding depreciation? When deciding whether to buy a new or used boat, you might wonder how depreciation plays a role in your choice. Depreciation is simply the reduction in value of a boat over time. Understanding how this affects new and used boats can guide you in making a smart decision. New boats usually depreciate faster than used ones. The moment you take delivery of a new boat, its value starts to drop. Typically, a new boat can lose about 10% to 15% of its value within the first year. This trend continues with the value decreasing by around 20% to 30% by the fifth year. By the time a boat reaches 10 years old, it may have lost about 30% to 40% of its original price. On the other hand, used boats have already gone through their steepest depreciation curve. This means their value tends to be more stable over time. Since the initial drop in value has already occurred, used boats often retain their worth better than new boats. This stability can be a big plus if you plan to sell the boat later. When it comes to fishing boats, your choice between new and used can depend on your specific needs. If having the latest technology and minimal maintenance is important to you, a new boat might be the way to go. New boats often come equipped with modern features that can enhance your fishing experience. However, if you are working with a tighter budget and want a cost-effective option, a used fishing boat can still offer reliable performance without the hefty price tag. Ultimately, whether it is better to buy a new or used boat regarding depreciation depends on your priorities. If you care about resale value and plan to keep the boat for a short time, a used boat may be a better fit. However, if you want the latest features and are okay with absorbing the initial depreciation, a new boat could be the right choice for you. Consider your needs, budget, and how long you plan to keep the boat to make the best decision for your fishing adventures.

- Yacht Racing 101: Kind: captions Language: en-US Yacht racing is a thrilling sport that blends skill, strategy, and teamwork on the water. Whether you are sailing a nimble dinghy or a powerful offshore yacht, every race is a test of precision and adaptability. Races can be short sprints or long-distance challenges, each with unique demands. Courses are marked by buoys, and the start sequence is signaled by flags and sounds. Understanding right-of-way rules is crucial to avoid penalties and ensure safe, fair competition. Proper gear is essential: sailing gloves, non-slip shoes, and a well-fitting life jacket are must-haves. Safety is always a priority, with teams carrying radios, flares, and practicing regular drills to handle emergencies on the water. Every crew member has a specific role, from steering to trimming sails. Clear communication and trust are vital, turning a group of sailors into a coordinated team. Even beginners can contribute by learning supportive roles and growing into more technical positions. New sailors practice tacking, jibing, and trimming sails to maximize speed. Reading wind shifts and balancing the boat are key skills that develop with experience and observation. Join a local sailing club or volunteer as crew to learn from experienced racers. With practice and mentorship, anyone can find their footing in yacht

- CHAPTER5 Networking in the Yacht World - Meet the right people: Kind: captions Language: en riding the wave mastering yacht interviews the yachting industry stands distinct not just because of its inherent luxury and charm but also due to its unique professional demands as such acing a yacht interview can be a bit different than conventional job interviews and preparing accordingly is crucial just like you wouldn't set sail without checking the weather you shouldn't dive into an interview without proper preparation this guide is here to ensure you ride the W with confidence and leave a lasting impression navigating different Waters common interview formats one face-to-face interviews this is the most traditional format and often preferred in the yachting industry as it allows for a more personal connection these can take place on the yacht at a yacht club or an office setting two over the phone while less personal phone interviews are not uncommon especially for preliminary discussions or when logistical challenges arise three video calls with technological advancements and the global nature of yachting video interviews via platforms like Zoom or Skype have become more prevalent they combine the convenience of phone interviews with the Personal Touch of face-to-face meetings charting the course typical interview questions and strategies to answer them one why are you interested in the yachting industry showcase genuine passion maybe it's the Allure of the Sea the intricacies of yachting operations or the chance to work with diverse clientele two what relevant experience do you bring to this position highlight experiences that showcase your technical abilities soft skills and adaptability don't just focus on tasks emphasize what you learned or how you added value three how do you handle challenging or high pressure situations narrate a real life instance where you faced a challenge how you addressed it and the outcome this not only provides proof of your problem solving skills but also demonstrates your ability to reflect and learn four have you ever had disagreements with crew members how did you handle it honesty is key if you did have disagreements describe them without casting blame focusing on the resolution and what you learned from the situation five what do you believe is the essence of excellent service on a luxury yacht dive into the intricacies of luxury service emphasizing attention to detail anticipating guest needs discretion and personalization six how do you stay updated with Maritime regulations safety protocols or any other relevant knowledge mention any courses you take forums or communities you're a part of or journals you read showcase a proactive approach to continuous learning anchoring the impression presenting oneself one dress code deck opt for smart casual attire a clean well-fitted polo shirt with chinos and deck shoes would be appropriate interior aim for a crisp professional look akin to what you'd wear serving guests a tailored dress or a shirt with slacks along with polished shoes works well Engineers while you don't need to be overly formal clean professional attire free of any grease or stains is essential two etiquette punctuality always arrive at least 10 minutes early it shows respect for the interviewer's time and showcases your reliability research familiarize yourself with the yacht or the company knowing specifics demonstrates genuine interest questions prepare some thoughtful questions about the role or the yacht this engagement can set you apart from other candidates three body language eye contact maintain steady eye contact it exudes confidence and attentiveness handshake a firm but not overpowering handshake can set a positive tone for the interview posture sit straight without appearing rigid it indicates attentiveness and professionalism Expressions while it's okay to be nervous remember to smile and engage age a warm demeanor can create a lasting positive impression the world of yachting while luxurious and enchanting demands Professionals of the highest caliber a yacht interview is not just about assessing skills but also about gauging fitment in a close-knit crew environment adaptability and commitment to Excellence by understanding the nuances of the interview process preparing diligently and presenting oneself appropriately aspirants can significantly increase their chances of charting a successful course in a glamorous world of yachting so set your compass prepare well and ride the wave to your dream yachting roll

- Boat Ownership EXPOSED!⚠️ The Costs Nobody Talks About: Kind: captions Language: en 54% of firsttime boat buyers end up selling their boats and leaving the hobby behind forever. And the reason has almost nothing to do with whether they actually enjoyed being out on the water. Sailors have a famous acronym they use for the word boat and it stands for breakout another thousand. That phrase first showed up on internet forums back in 1995. But here's the thing. Boers have since updated it to baft, which stands for break out another 5,000. Because even boat jokes aren't immune to inflation. What I'm going to do today is walk you through exactly what sailboat ownership actually costs in America. Covering the expenses that dealers conveniently forget to mention, the hidden fees that blindside nearly everyone, and the numbers that explain why so many people abandon boating after their first purchase. Whether you're thinking about buying a sailboat or you already own one and can't figure out where your money keeps disappearing [music] to, these numbers are going to make a lot of things suddenly make sense. The most important thing nobody tells new buyers is this. The purchase price of a sailboat represents only about 40 to 60% of what you'll actually spend in your first year of ownership. Take a 75,000 used sailboat and you can easily tack on an additional 15,000 to 40,000 in transaction costs, setup expenses, and firstear operating costs before the boat ever leaves the dock. That's not something going wrong with maintenance, and it's not bad luck catching up with you. It's simply what getting into sailing actually costs. Prices for entry-level boats in the 20 to 30 ft range cover an enormous spread. A new Bento First 24 starts around $75,000 and stepping up to a new 30-footer like the Bento Oceanis 30.1 puts you somewhere between $165,000 and $195,000. [music] The used market is where sensible buyers tend to look, though, and a well-maintained 10-year-old boat in that size range typically changes hands for $60,000 to $100,000. Go back 20 or 30 years to classics like the Catalina 22 and prices drop to 5,500 to $15,000. Mid-range cruising sailboats from 30 to 45 ft hit the sweet spot for coastal cruising and new production boats from builders like Jano, Bento, and Catalina run 200,000 to 350,000 fully equipped. Used options offer dramatically better value with respected bluewater cruisers like the Pacific Sea Craft 34 selling for 34,000 to 97,000 and the popular Catalina 36 marked the Sennon commanding $71,500 to $16,000. What dealers definitely won't mention is what Boatshed found after analyzing over 30,000 actual sales transactions. Boats depreciate about 15% per year during the first 5 years. Then that rate slows to 2 to 5% annually through year 10 and drops [music] to just 2.2% per year after that. And here's something else worth knowing. Actual sale prices average 22% below asking prices for sailboats, which means a boat listed at 100,000 will probably close somewhere around $78,000. Before you even take ownership, a series of transaction costs can add [music] 10,000 to 25,000 to whatever you agreed to pay for the boat. Marine surveys run 20 to 30 per foot for a pre-purchase condition and valuation inspection, which works out to $700 to pontoon for a 35-footer. The haul out gets build separately at $14 to $19 per foot, adding another $490 to 65 B just to pull the boat out of the water so someone can look at the bottom. An engine survey tax on roughly $500 more if you want one. Skipping the survey to save $1,000 ranks among the most expensive mistakes buyers make because a professional inspection can uncover 10,000 or more in hidden problems [music] while giving you real leverage during negotiations. Transport costs catch a lot of buyers offguard, especially when they find a great deal on a boat that's hundreds of miles away. Overland trucking for sailboats runs $7 to $10 per mile, which means a 5-mile delivery costs $3,500 to $5,000, and cross-country halls can easily reach $18,000 to $25,000. Mast removal and stepping adds $200 to $500 each way. On top of that, the expense that surprises people most though is state sales and use [music] tax. Depending on where you live, this ranges from 3 to 10% of the purchase price, which translates to an extra $3,950 to $7500 on a 75,000 boat that you might not have budgeted for at all. Annual ownership costs start with the expense that varies most dramatically depending on where you live, and that's marina slip fees. Geographic disparity in birthing costs represents one of ownership's biggest surprises. Marina slip fees range from $12 to 240 per foot annually across the country, averaging roughly $50 per foot per year nationwide. What that means for a 35- ft sailboat is annual slip costs anywhere from $420 to $8,400 depending entirely on your location. The Great Lakes region offers the most affordable birthing at $3,300 to $6,000 annually for a 35-footer, and that includes the required winter storage. Michigan State harbors charge as little as pad $64 per season for smaller boats. GF Coast marinas run 4,000 to 7,000. The Southeast cost 5,500 to 9,000 yearly, and premium West Coast and Northeast locations charge 4,000 to 11,000 annually. San Francisco Bay's Marina Del Rey charges up to 240 per foot per year, which is among the highest rates in the country. And there's another hidden factor in popular areas. Popular marinas in California and the Pacific Northwest maintain waiting lists of 2 to 10 years, which can force new owners into temporary and often more expensive births. Moorings cost 50 to 75% less than dock slips if you want to save money. With town moorings in Massachusetts running $300 to $600 per season versus marina slips at $6,300 to $8,400 for the same boat. The trade-off is you'll need a dinghy with an outboard which runs another $1,500 to $5,000. Insurance follows a straightforward formula. Just budget 1 to 1.5% of the boat's insured value annually. A $75,000 sailboat typically costs $750 to one at $120 filer per year for comprehensive coverage, though geography affects premiums significantly. Wisconsin boat owners pay an average of $261 annually, while Florida's hurricane exposure drives average premiums up to $812. [snorts] State registration fees vary from 25 to $250 annually and Coast Guard documentation costs $89 to $13 initially plus $26 per year for renewal. One thing to watch out for is third-party documentation services that charge $64 to $150 for paperwork you can handle yourself for just 26 directly through the USCG website. The 10% rule says you should budget 10% of your boat's purchase price annually for maintenance. And while that works as a starting point, a 2019 study of 33 boat owners found actual costs follow a slightly different pattern at roughly 7% plus a fixed baseline. What this means in practice is that cheaper boats actually require a higher percentage of their value to maintain. Owners of boats worth 2,000 to 5,000 reported spending 15 to 20% annually, while those with boats worth 40,000 or more spent closer to 5 to 10%. Where does all that maintenance money actually go? Bottom painting runs $150 to $250 for a 35-footer if you handle it yourself, or $450 to $700 if you hire it out. And you need this done every 1 to2 years in saltwater. The haul out alone costs $8 to $14 per foot, plus pressure washing at $4 to $12 per foot. So, budget $600 to 1,000 just for the round trip in and out of the water. Standing rigging needs replacement every 10 to 12 years in saltwater environments. And professional replacement costs 5,000 to 10,000 for a 30 to 40 ft boat. Running [music] rigging, meaning your sheets and howiards, lasts 5 to 10 years and costs 2,000 to 5,000 to replace. Quality Dacron cruising sales last 10 to 15 years under moderate use, but replacement costs for a 35-footer run 2,000 to 3500 for a main sale and 2,000 to 3500 for a Genoa. A full sale inventory totals 6,000 to 10,000. Engine maintenance costs $200 to 500 annually if you do the diesel service yourself or 500 to 2500 [music] if you pay someone. And a complete engine replacement runs 8,000 to 15,000 installed for a small diesel auxiliary. That replacement typically comes due after 5,000 to 10,000 hours or 20 plus years of use. Boatyard labor rates have climbed significantly and now average $100 to $180 per hour depending on region and specialty with Northeast and Pacific Northwest yards charging the highest rates at $125 to $180 per hour. The math gets uncomfortable for a lot of boat owners once you look at how much time they actually spend on the water. Industry data shows the average recreational boat operates just 50 to 100 hours annually. And Coast Guard data puts average usage at only 54 days per year at 100 hours of annual use with 5,000 in ownership costs. Each hour on the water [music] costs $50 before you add fuel or anything else. Demographics tell an interesting story about who's actually doing the boating these days. Median boat owner age reached 60 years old by the end of 2024 and more boat owners now fall into their 70s than their 40s. Infolink Technologies Jack Ellis put it this way in March 2025. Many of the people who owned boats 25 years ago are the same people who own boats today, but they're 25 years older. And that brings us back to the statistic from the beginning that 54% of firsttime boat buyers sell their boats and leave boating entirely. The reason they cite most often is unexpectedly high ownership costs. Some encouraging news exists for anyone thinking about buying right now because the pandemic era boat buying frenzy has definitively ended. 320,000 new power boats sold in 2020, marking a 13-year high and 415,000 firsttime buyers flooded into the market that year. But 2024 brought a 9.1% decline in new power boat sales, and year-to- date 2025 data through June shows a further 9.7% decline. On top of that, days on market has increased significantly across the board. New boats now spend 53 additional days on dealer lots compared to before. And year old boats sit 75 days longer than prepandemic norms. Boats group VP Courtney Chalmer's summed up what this means by saying somebody is going to have to come down on the price of the used boats. Industry forecasts anticipate used boat prices dropping 5 to 10% through 2025. And according to Boat US analysis, buyers now have more negotiation power than they've had during the last few years. All of these numbers add up to a clear picture of what sailboat ownership actually costs. 8,000 to 15,000 annually in slip fees, insurance, and routine maintenance is what a well-maintained 35- ft used sailboat purchased for 75,000 will realistically run you. Periodic major expenses like sales and rigging average another 2,000 to 5,000 per year when you spread them out over their replacement cycles. At typical usage rates of 50 to 100 hours per year, ownership costs alone translate to $100 to $200 per hour of actual sailing time. [music] And that doesn't even count what you paid for the boat itself. The purchase price really does function as [music] just the down payment on a relationship that demands continuous investment. But for those who sail frequently, [music] develop DIY maintenance skills, choose affordable regions, and properly budget for the inevitable expenses, [music] ownership can deliver experiences that you simply cannot get any other way. Just go in with your eyes open because boat doesn't stand for best of all times. It stands for break out another thousand or these days another 5,000. Thanks for watching.
