- How Much Does It Cost To Maintain A Yacht? - The Rich And Famous Files: Kind: captions Language: en How much does it cost to maintain a yacht? If you have ever wondered about the costs associated with maintaining a yacht, you are not alone. For the rich and famous, owning a yacht is not just about the initial purchase price. It comes with a whole range of ongoing expenses. Generally, yacht maintenance costs can range from about 10% to 20% of the yacht's value each year. This percentage can vary significantly, especially for larger and more complex vessels known as super yachts. Let's break it down with some examples. A superyacht valued at $500 million like Jeff Bezos sailing yacht Cororo can cost around $25 million annually just for maintenance and operation. This hefty sum covers crew salaries, fuel, docking fees, insurance, repairs, and routine upkeep. Cororo is a massive 127 m long and accommodates 18 guests with a crew of 36. Keeping such a large yacht in top shape requires extensive maintenance to ensure everything runs smoothly. Even smaller luxury yachts come with their own set of costs. Take a yacht worth $1,340,000. The annual maintenance for this size can be around $273,000. Fuel costs alone can run into tens of thousands of dollars each year depending on how often the yacht is used. For instance, Michael Jordan's $115 million super yacht reportedly costs about $10 million each year to maintain. This reflects the high costs associated with luxury features, a skilled crew, and the upkeep of such a large vessel. Now, let's look at the key components that contribute to yacht maintenance costs. First, crew salaries can be substantial. For larger yachts, crews can range from a dozen to several dozen professionals, including captains, engineers, chefs, and stewards. Next is fuel. Operating a yacht, especially a large one, consumes a lot of fuel, which can be quite expensive and varies based on usage. Then there are docking and birthing fees. Premium marina spots in popular locations can be costly adding to the overall expense. Routine maintenance is another significant factor. This includes engine servicing, hull cleaning, repainting, and replacing parts. Additionally, yachts must be periodically lifted out of the water for inspection and cleaning, which can cost tens of thousands of dollars. Insurance is also a major cost. High-V valueue yachts requiring comprehensive insurance policies that contribute to annual expenses for celebrities and wealthy individuals. These costs are part of maintaining a luxurious lifestyle and a status symbol. The expenses are often justified by the exclusivity, privacy, and prestige that yachts provide. Many super yachts are also chartered when not in personal use, which can help offset some of these maintenance costs. In summary, maintaining a yacht is a multi-million dollar annual commitment for the ultra wealthy. These ongoing costs ensure that the yacht remains in pristine condition and ready for travel anywhere in the world.

- Boat Loans 101: Should You Finance A Boat?: Kind: captions Language: en-US Okay, I’ll say it: buying a boat can&nbsp; be expensive. That’s why 70% of boaters&nbsp;&nbsp; choose to finance their boat. But what exactly is&nbsp; financing a boat, how can you prepare for it, and&nbsp;&nbsp; should you finance a boat? All of those questions&nbsp; - and more - will be covered in this video! Similar to other types of financing, boat&nbsp; loans are personal installment loans that&nbsp;&nbsp; are offered by lenders, who agree to lend you a&nbsp; fixed amount of money. From there, you pay the&nbsp;&nbsp; loan off (with interest) by the agreed date.&nbsp; The greatest difference, however, is that the&nbsp;&nbsp; interest rates are often higher for recreational&nbsp; vehicles than they are for your home or car. The average car loan interest rate currently&nbsp; hovers around 4%, while a recreational boat loan&nbsp;&nbsp; typically averages between 6 and 8%. Houses and&nbsp; cars are seen as essential assets - while boats&nbsp;&nbsp; are not. So, in the event of financial hardship,&nbsp; you would likely give up your boat before your car&nbsp;&nbsp; or house - making a boat loan a higher risk&nbsp; for the lender - therefore the higher rate. There are three different types of&nbsp; loans you can utilize for a boat:&nbsp;&nbsp; secured loans, unsecured&nbsp; loans, and a second mortgage. Secured Loans Secured loans are backed by collateral,&nbsp;&nbsp; such as a savings account or vehicle. This means&nbsp; that in the event the boat cannot be paid for,&nbsp;&nbsp; your lender can place a lien on your collateral,&nbsp; practically possessing it until the loan is paid&nbsp;&nbsp; off. Because this is a lower risk to the lender,&nbsp; interest rates are often lower for secured loans. Unsecured Loans&nbsp; On the other hand, with unsecured loans, your&nbsp; loan is not backed by collateral. Instead,&nbsp;&nbsp; approval for the loan is solely based on your&nbsp; credit score and finances. Rates are often higher&nbsp;&nbsp; for unsecured loans and lenders are typically more&nbsp; selective about who they would approve for a loan. Second Mortgage Second mortgages are an option for individuals&nbsp;&nbsp; who do not want to take out a personal loan. But&nbsp; here, your home serves as collateral. Of course,&nbsp;&nbsp; this is the riskiest loan option. Second mortgage&nbsp; loans also tend to have lower interest rates. Before you apply for a loan, it’s important to&nbsp; do a little research. This first step will help&nbsp;&nbsp; you understand if and what you will be able&nbsp; to finance. And that’s your credit score. CREDIT SCORE First, it’s important to know your credit&nbsp;&nbsp; score. This magic number will let you know if you&nbsp; are likely to be approved for a recreational loan.&nbsp;&nbsp; Since recreational loans are a higher risk for&nbsp; lenders, they’re also more selective with what&nbsp;&nbsp; credit scores they will accept for loans.&nbsp; Generally, you’ll need a credit score above&nbsp;&nbsp; 600 to be approved for a boat loan, although&nbsp; we’ve seen people with lower scores be approved. Additionally, knowing your credit score can&nbsp; help you approximate your interest rate,&nbsp;&nbsp; which we’ll use in a couple of&nbsp; minutes for payment calculations.&nbsp;&nbsp; A better credit score equals&nbsp; a lower interest rate. There are many great options for you to check&nbsp; your credit score - for free! I use Credit Karma.&nbsp;&nbsp; Credit Karma uses a slightly different grading&nbsp; model to determine your credit score, however.&nbsp;&nbsp; They use “VantageScore” to generate credit&nbsp; scores, where most lenders will be looking at&nbsp;&nbsp; your FICO score. Credit Karma’s scores should&nbsp; closely reflect your credit score, but it may&nbsp;&nbsp; not be the final score your lenders see - and&nbsp; ultimately use to determine your loan approval. Another important factor that lenders&nbsp; will assess is your debt-to-income&nbsp;&nbsp; ratio and employment/housing stability. * Your debt-to-income ratio – or DTI – is&nbsp;&nbsp; the percentage of your income that you devote&nbsp; to paying off debts, which include but are not&nbsp;&nbsp; limited to: mortgage payments, credit cards,&nbsp; student loans, auto loans, and personal loans.&nbsp;&nbsp; Rent payments, although they’re not technically&nbsp; “debt”, are also included in your DTI.&nbsp;&nbsp; Boat loans will likely be denied if&nbsp; your DTI is higher than 40 or 45%&nbsp;&nbsp; once the new boat loan is factored in. * As far as employment and housing&nbsp;&nbsp; stability goes, the longer you've lived in the&nbsp; same area and worked for the same employers,&nbsp;&nbsp; the more favorably a lender is likely&nbsp; to look at you for a boat loan approval. PRE-APPROVAL Now that you know your credit score,&nbsp;&nbsp; DTI, and stability status, it’s important to see&nbsp; if you can get pre-approved for a loan. All the&nbsp;&nbsp; time you spend researching and testing out boats&nbsp; will be for naught if you can’t get approved! Marine dealerships - such as ourselves - have&nbsp; in-house finance applications which are then sent&nbsp;&nbsp; to their various lending partners. So, you can&nbsp; do it that way or manually apply for pre-approval&nbsp;&nbsp; yourself. You’d have to individually go through&nbsp; different lenders and assess each offer. Many lenders require a minimum down payment,&nbsp;&nbsp; which often ranges between 10% and&nbsp; 20% of the loan’s value. However,&nbsp;&nbsp; there are plenty of lenders that offer lower&nbsp; down payment rates. Regardless, plan accordingly. APPROVAL & Comparison Alright - now you’ve&nbsp;&nbsp; got a couple of offers, but need to&nbsp; decide which option is right for you.&nbsp;&nbsp; Let’s pretend you’re wanting to&nbsp; buy this SunTracker PartyBarge 22,&nbsp;&nbsp; which will cost you around $50,000 -&nbsp; including a trailer, prep, and freight. When you’re comparing lender offers, it’s&nbsp; best to choose the offer with the lowest rate,&nbsp;&nbsp; fewest fees, and repayment terms that align with&nbsp; your budget. The longer-term length of the loan,&nbsp;&nbsp; the lower the monthly payment. However, interest&nbsp; rates can be higher for longer-term lengths,&nbsp;&nbsp; so you’ll be accruing additional interest that&nbsp; way. Vice versa: the shorter the term, the higher&nbsp;&nbsp; the monthly payments - but you’ll be paying&nbsp; less in total interest at the end of the term. Now’s the time to do some calculations.&nbsp; Assuming we take out a $50,000 loan and&nbsp;&nbsp; pay the boat off over 15 years with a 6% interest&nbsp; rate, we’d pay $421.93 a month. If, for example,&nbsp;&nbsp; we had a lower credit score, which - in turn&nbsp; - raises the interest rate to - let’s say&nbsp;&nbsp; 8% - the monthly payment jumps to $477.83 . Point&nbsp; being, keeping your score healthy can really&nbsp;&nbsp; help you manage monthly finances and even buy a&nbsp; bigger dream boat with the same monthly payment! Here’s a little tip: if you’re able&nbsp; to, put additional money down when you&nbsp;&nbsp; can throughout the term. If we just put $50 a&nbsp; month towards the initial loan we talked about,&nbsp;&nbsp; you will end up paying off your boat 2&nbsp; years sooner, plus save extra on interest!&nbsp;&nbsp; Putting additional cash towards your&nbsp; boat over the term is a great way&nbsp;&nbsp; to knock off some cash you would&nbsp; have otherwise spent on interest. So - should you finance a boat? If you’re&nbsp; not able to pay off the boat in full,&nbsp;&nbsp; then of course. Financing a boat allows you to&nbsp; pay for the boat monthly and control how long the&nbsp;&nbsp; loan term is. Just remember: the lower the rate&nbsp; and shorter the term, the more money you save! Thank you for watching this&nbsp; video. Check out this video&nbsp;&nbsp; about the true cost of owning a boat right here! Leave us any questions you may have or&nbsp; recommendations for videos we should make&nbsp;&nbsp; down the line below in the comments section!&nbsp; Again - thank you. For Foothills Marine,&nbsp;&nbsp; I’m Mason Strother and I’ll see&nbsp; you in the next Learning Hub Video.

- Your Ultimate Guide to Yacht Financing in Singapore: Kind: captions Language: en if you are thinking of buying a yacht in Singapore you might be wondering how to finance your purchase there are different options available depending on your budget preferences and eligibility in this video we will explore some of the most common ways to finance a yacht in Singapore and provide some tips and advice to help you make the best decision one of the most straightforward ways to finance a yacht is to pay cash this means that you have enough savings or liquid assets to cover the full cost of the y including taxes fees and maintenance paying cash has some advantages such as avoiding interest charges having full ownership of the yacht and having more bargaining power with the seller however paying cash also has some drawbacks such as tying up a large amount of capital losing the opportunity to invest in other assets and exposing yourself to Market fluctuations and depreciation another option to finance a yacht is to take a loan this means that you borrow money from a bank or a financial institution to pay for the yacht and repay the loan over a period of time with interest taking a loan has some advantages such as spreading the cost of the yacht over time preserving your cash flow and benefiting from tax deductions on interest payments however taking a loan also has some drawbacks such as paying more than the original price of the yacht having to meet the lender's requirements and criteria and risking repossession of the yacht if you def fault on the loan a third option to finance a yacht is to lease it this means that you rent The Yacht from a leasing company for a fixed period of time and pay a monthly fee that covers the depreciation maintenance and insurance of the yacht leasing a yacht has some advantages such as having lower upfront costs having more flexibility in choice and having the option to buy the yacht at the end of the lease however leasing a yacht also has some drawbacks such as paying more than the market value of the yacht having limited control and customization of the yacht and having to abide by the terms and conditions of the lease a fourth option to finance a yacht is to join a yacht club or a yacht sharing program this means that you become a member of a club or a program that owns and operates a fleet of yachts and pay a membership fee that gives you access to use the Yachts for a certain number of days per year joining a yacht club or a yacht sharing program has some advantages such as having lower costs having more variety and convenience and having less hassle and responsibility however joining a yacht club or a yacht sharing program also has some drawbacks such as having limited availability and flexibility having to share the yacht with other members and having to follow the rules and regulations of the club or the program as you can see there are different ways to finance a yacht in Singapore and each one has its pros and cons the best option for you depends on your personal situation preferences and goals before you decide you should do your research compare the options and   1. @xfernandez11 [7 likes]: Wow! So much packed into one video, but it is all spot on! I’m on my second liveaboard boat and everything she says is true. Take notes!
  2. @Haladmer [5 likes]: Something else to note, in boating, age of the hull often has a huge impact on a lot of other factors.  If your hull is 10-15yo and had sails, insurance is going to basically mandate new rigging (or at the least a very thorough rigging inspection by a highly accredited inspection team), even without sails,  insurance brokers are starting to shy away from boats over 15yo, so insurance will be a premium.  Over 20yo boats are damned near impossible in the US to insure for anything other than liability now.

For mooring/berths, a lot of marinas are fine if your boat is older, as long as it is "clean" (presentable, no visible damage/leaks/etc...), but over 10yo, and expect to provide recent (and time/date stamped) photos.  Most marinas now also require proof of at least liability insurance to tie-off, and there are some that won't take in boats 20yo+ for anything other than "transit" berthing.

Finally, if you're planning to liveaboard, know that marinas have been cutting back on available slips across the board, and in popular areas, you can be facing a wait list of months to 10+ years.  For example, the SF area's Pier 39 marina has frozen their waitlist because it was well over a decade long with no hope of it clearing in the immediate future.

Boating is great, and boat ownership is exciting at the start, but as the video said, be realistic in your plans and expectations, emergencies on boats are not like life ashore, you will often only have yourself and YouTube videos for support underway.  When you're in port though, people crawl out from every stone and barnacle to offer advice, as long as you're not "that person".  If you don't know what I mean, you'll often know them almost immediately when you meet them lol

- Legally Slash $600K Taxes with This Yacht Secret!: Kind: captions Language: en I had a successful entrepreneur come to me making about $3 million a year. I'm getting killed in taxes. What can I do? It's a vacation. It's a money pit. And if you treat it that way, that's exactly what it'll be. You'll pay for it with after tax dollars. Every expense, fuel, maintenance, taxes, insurance, and the crew will come straight out of your pocket. The wealthy CFO, the rich dad, reframes this differently. They don't buy a yacht, they buy a marine charter business that just happens to operate a yacht. So, what helps make this strategy so powerful now? Well, the one big beautiful bill act increase the amount that we can depreciate under section 179 and bonus depreciation. And that bill made huge changes. It took the amount that you're able to appreciate under 179 from 1.25 million to 2.5 million in one year. So, if you buy a piece of qualified business expense and a properly structured yacht would qualify, you can write off up to $2.5 million in the first year. Now, this does have a catch. If you're purchasing assets of $4 million or more, it does begin to phase out. But for many, that's a massive upfront tax deduction. But what if the yacht cost you more or with all of your purchases, you're over the phase out amount? That's where the 100% bonus depreciation comes into play. It's not an election. There's no limit. For qualified business property, you can write off up to 100% of the asset purchases for the year. And this is for new or used assets. Now, let's look at a real life example. Let's say that you're buying a $ 1.5 million yacht. Under section 179, you can take the full $1.5 million deduction, or you could use section 179 for the first million, bonus depreciation for the remaining half million. The nice thing is there are alternatives to be able to maximize the deductions you're entitled to in the year utilizing these two IRS code sections. In the end result, you have a $1.5 million business deduction that you didn't have before. If your effective tax rate is 40%, that's $600,000 of tax savings that you're able to generate in the current year. That's additional cash that you can deploy to put into your business and help increase your net worth. So, what's happening? The government is helped financing your yacht. Are you seeing this? We just created $600,000 in cash flow for you utilizing a tax strategy. It's important to remember that most CPAs and tax preparers are like reporters. They report what happened the last year and put it on your taxes. We're more like architects. We build you strategies for your future. If I'm bringing you ideas that may make you a little nervous and excited all at the same time, you probably have the wrong CPA. Book a call with my team with the link below. We're the architects. Let's design you a blueprint. Okay, this all sounds incredible, but it's important to understand with deductions comes regulations. We have to make sure that we're adhering to the IRS regulations. So you retain the deductions you're entitled to. And sometimes this is where people get lazy. And when they get lazy, if there something is audited, they can't substantiate the deduction and they lose it, which can be very, very expensive. The first rule we want to talk about is a 50% test. You have to use this asset for 50% or more for business use. This is a non-negotiable. The yacht must be used for business purpose, 50% or more. The usage is measured in days. business days, personal days. With personal use, you get the greater of 14 days or 10% of the days rented. That's it. Go over that threshold, you no longer have a deduction for the year. It's gone. And you need proof. It's critical to document any deduction that you have, especially for larger ones like this. You need a log that you use to track whenever you have business usage for it to be able to substantiate who was on the yacht, what the business purpose was, and how much revenue was generated. And just like any other business, you need a separate bank account, marketing plan, documents to be able to substantiate that this is a true business venture. Now, let's go over rule number two, hobby loss rules. The IRS is expecting you to have a profit in three out of five years with business ventures or they could come back in and consider it a hobby. If this happens and they come back and classify it as a hobby, they can eliminate the deductions you've taken in the past. So, we don't want to have a call back. We'll make sure we got a proper business plan and a way for you to be able to make money with your yacht business. And with businesses that take losses, especially the one that you're going to have in the first year, material participation rules are extremely important. If you're considered to be passive in the charter business, then any losses that you have would only offset passive income. Business owners typically are generating ordinary income and need ordinary losses to help offset that. So, how do you make sure this is structured correctly to be able to meet the material participation rules? There's seven rules for material participation. You only need to meet one of these. Let's go over the three most common ones. The 500 hour test. You materially participate for 500 hours out of the year. That's about 10 hours a week. Managing the marketing, approving charters, overseeing maintenance. This is something that's doable. The substantially all test. Your participation is substantially all of the participation involved for the activity for the year. And the 100 hour test. You put in a 100 hours more than anybody else puts in on the business for the year. This is a critical piece of the strategy. You cannot be an absentee owner. You need to look at this business and make sure that you can meet one of the material participation rules to make this active loss and not a passive loss for you. The charter yacht strategy is an incredibly powerful strategy, but it's only one of the strategies we have available for our clients. It's Sunday the 20th of July, a 11:45 in&nbsp; the morning. We have just about an hour ago&nbsp;&nbsp; crossed the Norwegian territorial waters, the 12&nbsp;mile zone, and we're inbound. And we can&nbsp;&nbsp; see land now. The sun is out, the wind is&nbsp; off. I think we're going to take the sails&nbsp;&nbsp; down now because they're just flapping and wearing on the whole structure. So that's&nbsp;&nbsp; the plan. We have another 46 nautical&nbsp; miles to go to Tromsø and our ETA is about&nbsp;&nbsp; uh 6 to 7 in the evening. So we're almost done.&nbsp; Everything went well so far. Now we just need&nbsp;&nbsp; to get it through the last couple of nautical&nbsp; miles. It's really summer here now. The sun is&nbsp;&nbsp; out and it's 15°C. I don't know when last we had&nbsp; 15° C. That was probably about a year ago or so&nbsp;&nbsp; that's quite strange. So soon I will&nbsp; have to put on my shorts and t-shirt. Summer! It's okay. It's nice. It's&nbsp; summer. It's almost 20° C and&nbsp;&nbsp; time to get some sun. Time to get some suntan. Nice t-shirt – from Ny-Ålesund... Okay, so take this: 555 nautical miles&nbsp; ago, I said we got to be here in this&nbsp;&nbsp; tidal gate around 3 to 4 to 5 (o'clock), not before&nbsp; 3 and not after 7 to get through. Now,&nbsp;&nbsp; guess where we are? We are in the plain&nbsp; middle of it (the tidal current zone), it's pulling us through with&nbsp;&nbsp; 3 knots. And we didn't have to stop or&nbsp; to accelerate. We just bang on that seammanship. We are not sponsored, but it's okay. But I've eaten much better on this&nbsp; trip than this. Yeah. But we just bought this&nbsp;&nbsp; because it's easy to make because&nbsp; you never know. Yes. What happens? No, it's okay. As we sail back into the familiar waters&nbsp; of Tromsø, 33 days after we first cast off,&nbsp;&nbsp; the journey begins to replay in our minds.&nbsp; This trip took us far beyond our comfort zones.&nbsp;&nbsp; At times it was physically demanding, at times&nbsp; mentally challenging. The highs were very high and&nbsp;&nbsp; the lows could feel just as deep. But every mile&nbsp; north brought something extraordinary. We reached&nbsp;&nbsp; the remote and mysterious island of Bjørnøya with&nbsp; its lovely weather station and long quiet history.&nbsp;&nbsp; We sailed among drifting icebergs. We watched a&nbsp; polar bear sleeping on the shore and walrus resting&nbsp;&nbsp; heavily along the coast. In Svalbard, we saw sharp&nbsp; contrasts. The lively streets of Longyearbyen full&nbsp;&nbsp; of visitors and activity. Barentsburg, the&nbsp; old Russian mining town that still feels&nbsp;&nbsp; frozen in the Soviet era. And Ny-Ålesund,&nbsp; a quiet international research station today,&nbsp;&nbsp; but also a former mining town. And a place where&nbsp; Roald Amenson began his airship journey to the&nbsp;&nbsp; North Pole. Three very different communities,&nbsp; all built on coal, but shaped in their own way&nbsp;&nbsp; by life in the high Arctic. Everywhere we looked,&nbsp; the past was still present. Old hunting stations,&nbsp;&nbsp; abandoned ventures, reminders of human ambition,&nbsp; preserved by the cold and the dry Arctic air, left&nbsp;&nbsp; untouched for decades, sometimes centuries. But&nbsp; what stayed with us most was the landscape itself,&nbsp;&nbsp; the raw beauty of it. jacket snowcapped mountains&nbsp; rising straight from the sea. Endless glaciers,&nbsp;&nbsp; small signs of life holding on in a harsh and&nbsp; fragile environment. We sailed through strong&nbsp;&nbsp; winds and thick fog, through drizzle and bright&nbsp; sunshine. And yes, we burned a fair bit of diesel&nbsp;&nbsp; along the way. And now, as the lines are tight&nbsp; and the engine finally falls silent in Tromsø,&nbsp;&nbsp; one feeling rises above the rest: Gratitude.&nbsp; Gratitude that Polaris brought us safely home.&nbsp;&nbsp; And gratitude that we were able to experience&nbsp; a small part of this remarkable corner of the&nbsp;world. But our sailing season is far from over&nbsp; yet, and we enjoy an extraordinary autumn here&nbsp;&nbsp; in the far north. Join us next time when we&nbsp; embark on a tour outside the islands of Senya&nbsp;&nbsp; and Vesterålen before we return to our safe&nbsp; winter harbour ahead of the Arctic winter storms.

- DO NOT Pay Cash For A Boat Until You Watch THIS! | BoatUS: Kind: captions Language: en Hi I'm Mike Vatalaro with BoatUS.&nbsp; I wanted to do this followup to&nbsp;&nbsp; our Price vs. Cost video to address one&nbsp; of the most frequent comments we received.&nbsp;&nbsp; If you haven't seen it pop on over to the&nbsp; BoatUS YouTube channel and check it [Music] out. One of the more frequent comments was some&nbsp; variation of "If you can't pay cash for it, you&nbsp;&nbsp; can't afford it." Now let me just start by saying,&nbsp; I 100% agree you shouldn't be buying things you&nbsp;&nbsp; can't afford, whether it's boats, cars, other&nbsp; luxury items. But it is a huge leap from "If you&nbsp;&nbsp; can't pay cash, you can't afford it" to "Paying&nbsp; cash for those items is best." Let me explain.&nbsp;&nbsp; The idea that paying in full for a big ticket item&nbsp; is best ignores the opportunity cost of tying your&nbsp;&nbsp; dollars up in that asset. Opportunity cost is&nbsp; just a phrase that means what's the highest and&nbsp;&nbsp; best use of that dollar, now or in the future.&nbsp; Let's go back to the example in the first video:&nbsp;&nbsp; Our buyer in the first video spent $440,000 on&nbsp; a boat in 2013. We know exactly what that costs.&nbsp;&nbsp; But what if, instead, they had put $4,000 down&nbsp; and financed the balance? What would that have&nbsp;&nbsp; looked like? So at the interest rates available&nbsp; in 2013, I calculated a monthly payment of about&nbsp;&nbsp; $381 a month for 10 years. That added about&nbsp; $9,700 in finance charges to the overall cost&nbsp;&nbsp; of that boat. So why is this opportunity better?&nbsp; It comes down to what other opportunities did&nbsp;&nbsp; our buyer have to do with that $36,000? Well,&nbsp; if they had simply stuck it in the S&P 500,&nbsp;&nbsp; what would have happened? The cumulative total&nbsp; return for that decade was 167%, meaning that&nbsp;&nbsp; $36,000 would have grown to $96,000. So our buyer&nbsp; missed out on approximately $60,000 in gains,&nbsp;&nbsp; which more than offsets the $9,700 they would have&nbsp; spent in financing. Now I'm not saying everybody&nbsp;&nbsp; should finance everything. That's a recipe for&nbsp; a cash-flow disaster. What I am saying is you&nbsp;&nbsp; can benefit from borrowing as long as your money&nbsp; is outgrowing your borrowing costs. Now there's&nbsp;&nbsp; plenty of reasons to avoid monthly payments. Maybe&nbsp; you're self-employed and your income fluctuates.&nbsp;&nbsp; Maybe you have seasonal employment. And perhaps,&nbsp; more commonly ,maybe your credit doesn't allow&nbsp;&nbsp; you to access the low interest rates necessary to&nbsp; have gains in the stock market that offset those&nbsp;&nbsp; costs. Now, this assumes that you're comfortable&nbsp; with the risks inherit in the stock market. If&nbsp;&nbsp; you are one of those stuffing-under-the-mattress&nbsp; people, this isn't going to work for you. Listen,&nbsp;&nbsp; there's no better feeling than knowing something&nbsp; is bought and paid for that you own it outright.&nbsp;&nbsp; That is a very good feeling! I just don't want you&nbsp; to spend too much money getting there. Let's keep&nbsp;&nbsp; the conversation going in the comments. Please&nbsp; like and subscribe -- and we'll see you out there!

- How Is A Private Yacht Dry-docked For Hull Repair? - Luxury Life Report: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] How is a private yacht dry docked forhole repair? Imagine your luxury yacht resting peacefully on a set of sturdy supports out of the water, ready for a thorough checkup. That's what happens during dry docking forhole repair. The process begins as the yacht arrives at a specialized shipyard equipped with the right facilities. Depending on the size of the vessel, it might be placed in a dry dock that is either a large basin that can be drained or a floating platform that can be submerged and raised. For smaller yachts, a large crane called a travel lift is used to lift the vessel onto a hard stand on land. Before the yacht enters the dry dock, a detailed plan is created. This plan marks where the supporting blocks will go to cradle the hole securely. These blocks are custom made to fit the shape of the yacht's hull, ensuring even weight distribution and avoiding any damage. Once everything is set, the yacht is carefully positioned and the dock is sealed. Water is then pumped out slowly over several hours, allowing the yacht to settle onto the supports. Underwater technicians or divers double check the placement of the supports to ensure everything is perfectly aligned. With the yacht out of the water, the real work begins. The hull is inspected for damage like scratches, corrosion, or marine growth such as algae and barnacles. These can affect the yacht's performance and fuel efficiency. The whole surface is cleaned using highpress water jets or abrasive blasting to remove old paint and fouling. Repairs follow, which may include fixing cracks, restoring the gel coat, or replacing damaged fiberglass. The propellers, shafts, rudders, and C valves are also checked and serviced or replaced if needed. After repairs, the hull is primed and coated with specialized marine paints, including antifouling paints that prevent marine growth and protect the surface. These coatings are chosen for their durability and appearance, keeping the yacht looking pristine. During the process, safety and quality control are top priorities. Inspectors from classification societies or flag authorities often oversee the work to ensure it meets safety standards. The entire dry docking can take from a few days for minor repairs to several weeks for extensive work depending on the yacht size and needs. Once all repairs are complete, the yard slowly refills the dry dock with water. Tugboats assist in guiding the yacht out of the dock. Sea trials then follow to test propulsion, steering, and other systems. When everything checks out, the yacht is returned to service, looking and performing at its best. For owners, this process isn't just maintenance. It's a way to preserve their investment, keeping the yacht in perfect condition for years to come. It's a blend of craftsmanship, premium materials, and careful planning to maintain the elegance, safety, and value of a private vessel.

- The True Cost of Owning a Luxury Yacht: Kind: captions Language: en Welcome aboard, luxury lovers and sea dreamers. If you've ever fantasized about sailing across crystal blue waters on your very own luxury yacht surrounded by elegance, privacy, and the open ocean, you're not alone. A luxury yacht isn't just a boat. It's a symbol of ultimate freedom and wealth. But before you pop the champagne and start planning your Mediterranean voyage, there's something important to understand. Owning a yacht comes with a hefty price tag that extends far beyond the initial purchase. From hidden operating costs to ongoing maintenance and crew salaries, the true cost of owning a luxury yacht might surprise you. In today's video, we're diving deep into the world of luxury yacht ownership. We'll explore how much it really costs to buy and maintain a yacht, what the operating expenses look like year-over-year, how far these vessels can travel, and what kind of lavish features you can expect on board. We'll also touch on financing options, life on board, and whether it's worth owning outright or considering alternatives like chartering. So, grab your captain's hat and buckle up. We're about to explore the realities of life at sea in true luxury. [Music] Let's start with the most obvious cost. Buying the yacht itself. Luxury yachts come in many sizes and styles, and their prices can vary drastically depending on the brand, size, materials, and features. On the lower end of the luxury spectrum, you might find a 60 ft yacht for around $1.5 million to $3 million. These are often considered entry-level super yachts capable of coastal cruising and short expeditions in considerable comfort. They still come equipped with high-end amenities, sleek interiors, and advanced navigation systems. As we move up in scale, the prices begin to soar. A 100 ft yacht from a respected builder like Sunseeker or Princess may cost between $8 million and $15 million. These vessels typically offer multiple guest cabins, luxurious owner suites, expansive salons, and even small gyms or theaters. They're designed for longer journeys and entertaining guests in style, making them popular among celebrities and successful entrepreneurs. The level of craftsmanship and technology at this size also begins to rival that of small boutique hotels. But the real giants, those 200 f feet and beyond, are in a league of their own. These mega yachts are often fully custombuilt and can cost anywhere from $50 million to $500 million, depending on complexity and opulence. Builders like Len, Oceano, and Feedship are known for creating these floating mansions for the ultra wealthy. These yachts often include features like indoor pools, beach clubs, helilopipads, elevators, and even IMAX theaters. Essentially, they are luxury resorts that happen to be seaorthy. Customization is a major cost driver in yacht construction. Owners who want something truly unique can spend tens of millions more on specialized features. A helilopad might add3 million to $5 million, while an onboard spa or Turkish hamom could cost even more. Some owners commission artwork and rare materials throughout their vessels, driving prices higher. Every detail, from interior design to navigation systems, is tailored to the owner's desires, making each yacht a one-of-a-kind masterpiece. Finally, there's the question of resale. Unlike real estate, yachts tend to depreciate relatively quickly. Within just 5 years, a luxury yacht can lose up to 40% to 50% of its original value, especially if trends shift or if the yacht becomes outdated. Maintenance, wear and tear, and technological obsolescence also play a role. For many owners, the joy of the lifestyle outweighs concerns over resale, but it's an important financial consideration nonetheless. Yacht ownership isn't just about the purchase. It's also about understanding the life cycle and long-term investment reality. Now, let's talk about what many firsttime buyers don't consider, the operating costs. On average, the annual cost of running a luxury yacht ranges from 10% to 20% of its original purchase price. So for a $10 million yacht, you're looking at roughly $1 million to $2 million every year just to keep it operational. This includes crew salaries, fuel, docking fees, insurance, maintenance, repairs, food, and provisioning. Crew is often the single biggest expense. A typical luxury yacht might require a captain, engineer, deck hands, stewardist, and a chef, often working year round. For a midsize yacht, this can amount to $300,000 to $800,000 per year in salaries alone. Megaay yachts might employ over 20 crew members, pushing annual crew costs well above $2 million. These are experienced professionals managing complex onboard systems and delivering high-end service, and they're paid accordingly. Fuel is another major expense, especially for yachts that travel long distances. A 150 foot yacht might burn 500 gallons of diesel per hour at cruising speed. At an average cost of $4 to $6 per gallon, just one hour of cruising can cost upwards of $3,000 in fuel. If you're planning extended journeys, the fuel budget can easily hit hundreds of thousands of dollars annually. Maintenance and upkeep are non-negotiable for safety and reliability. Routine engine checks, hole cleaning, electronic servicing, and repainting all require time, money, and skilled labor. Expect to spend 2% to 4% of the yacht's value on maintenance alone each year. And don't forget about insurance, which can run from $30,000 to $500,000 annually, depending on the vessel's value and cruising areas. Luxury yachts are made for exploring the world. But how far can they actually travel? That depends heavily on size, design, and fuel capacity of the vessel. Smaller luxury yachts around 60 to 80 ft may have a range of 300 to 800 nautical miles. They're best suited for coastal cruising or short island hops. Perfect for exploring the Bahamas, Greek Isles, or French Riviera. Larger super yachts, particularly those in the 100 to 150 foot range, often have transatlantic capabilities. These boats can cruise 3,000 to 4,000 nautical miles without needing to refuel, opening up destinations like Europe to the Caribbean or the US to Southeast Asia. They're built for long range cruising with stabilizers for comfort and advanced navigation systems for safety. When you enter the mega yacht category, 200 f feet and above, you'll find vessels with truly global reach. Some can travel more than 6,000 nautical miles on a single tank. These yachts are essentially floating cities built across oceans in complete luxury, often with satellite communications, climate controlled storage, and backup systems to handle months at sea. Cruising speed typically ranges from 12 to 18 knots for most luxury yachts. Some sportier models can push beyond 25 knots, but speed comes at the expense of fuel efficiency. Long range cruising is done at lower speeds to conserve fuel and reduce wear in the engines. Ultimately, these yachts are designed not just for transportation, but for enjoying the journey as much as a destination. One of the main reasons people invest in luxury yachts is for the lifestyle they offer. Imagine waking up in a king-size bed to the sound of waves lapping against the hull, walking to the aft deck for breakfast prepared by your private chef, then diving into your infinity pool or heading out for a jet ski ride. On a yacht, everyday feels like a five-star vacation tailored to your preferences. Onboard amenities vary widely, but top tier yachts can include multiple guest suites, owner's decks, sky lounges, beach clubs, and even underwater observation rooms. Many have fully equipped gyms, movie theaters, massage rooms, and saunas. For those who enjoy entertaining, some yachts feature dance floors, full bars, live music setups, and private wine sellers. It's your floating mansion with every indulgence at your fingertips. Activities go far beyond sunbathing and sipping champagne. Yachts come equipped with a vast array of water toys, jet skis, kayaks, paddle boards, inflatable slides, diving gear, and more. Some carry tenders for fishing or fing guests to shore. Others feature helilopads allowing you to fly to and from the yacht wherever you are. Whether it's exploring secluded coes, snorkeling coral reefs, or dining on a remote island, the possibilities are endless. Living aboard full-time is also a growing trend. Some owners use their yachts as floating second homes or mobile offices. With high-speed satellite internet, private quarters, and business facilities, it's entirely possible to live and work from your yacht while sailing around the world. This flexibility and freedom is part of what makes yacht ownership so attractive to the ultra wealthy. Given the enormous cost involved, most yacht owners don't just write a check. They finance or structure their ownership through specialized channels. Traditional marine financing is available, especially for yachts under $20 million. Banks may require 20% to 30% down and interest rates typically range from 4% to 7% depending on credit and asset value. Repayment terms can span 5 to 15 years. For larger yachts, financing is often handled through private wealth firms or even leasing companies. Some buyers set up offshore ownership structures for tax efficiency or liability protection. Holding the yacht through a corporation or trust is common, especially for international owners. These structures can also help reduce VAT exposure when purchasing or registering the yacht. Chartering is another strategy to offset cost. Owners can list their yacht with charter brokers when they're not using it, earning income that covers a portion of annual expenses. A large yacht can command $200,000 to $500,000 per week in charter fees depending on size and season. Of course, chartering brings additional wear and tear and requires more intensive crew and management oversight. Fractional ownership has also entered the yaching world, allowing multiple individuals to co-own a yacht and split operating costs. This model appeals to those who want the luxury of yaching without the full-time responsibility or expense. Each co-owner gets a certain number of weeks per year on board, and management companies handle scheduling, crewing, and maintenance. Yachts are built to last, but like any complex asset, they require regular attention and eventually replacement. A well-maintained yacht can operate effectively for 20 to 30 years or more, but the first major overhaul usually comes after about 10 years. This might include engine refits, electronics upgrades, interior redesign, or hole repainting costing millions depending on the scope. Technological advances also drive update. New fuel efficiency systems, hybrid propulsion, advanced navigation, and entertainment systems all evolve quickly. Owners who want to stay current often refit their yachts every few years, not just for aesthetics, but for functionality. Failing to keep up with these updates can hurt resale value and increase operating risks. Depreciation is a significant factor in yacht ownership. New yachts lose value faster than used ones, especially in the first 5 years. To preserve value, regular maintenance, cosmetic upkeep, and usage logs are essential. Resale can be a challenge, especially if the yacht has highly specific customizations that may not appeal to future buyers. Eventually, most owners sell or trade in their yachts for a newer or larger model. Brokers handle these transactions, marketing the yacht to global buyers and managing the paperwork. For owners who no longer want the financial or time burden, selling the yacht marks the end of an incredible but costly journey on the seas. Owning a luxury yacht is the ultimate symbol of wealth and freedom, offering an unmatched lifestyle of privacy, exploration, and indulgence on the open seas. But it comes at a steep price. Beyond the multi-million dollar purchase, the ongoing cost of fuel, crew, maintenance, insurance, and depreciation quickly add up, turning a dream vessel into a serious financial commitment. Yet, for those with the means and the passion, the rewards are extraordinary. The ability to travel the world in comfort, host unforgettable experiences, and live life entirely on your own terms.

So unless you are very, very rich and can afford someone to always do all your maintenance and check things for you, and service everything, and do all the repairs - you're gonna have to do it yourself. But even if you are very rich things go wrong out there, they go wrong out at sea. Engine stops, sails break, you will need to know what to do. You must be prepared to do that. Are you prepared to do that, are you prepared to take on all these new skills? Boats kill relationships. Yeah, definitely. When you're living in the small confines of a boat everything is magnified. So if there are underlying problems in your relationship, they will come to the fore, believe me, when you are spending 24/7 with each other. Yeah, and the other thing to remember is when you start this life is you both have to be a hundred percent committed. It's no good if one of you is dragging the other into this life, because those cracks appear pretty quickly, and there are boats all over the world that were owned by couples who gave it all up. Yeah and we've seen many boats for sale, haven't we, because of this. You know, couples have split up, and sadly they've had to sell the dream. Yeah, there are a lot of solo sailors out there who weren't original solo sailors. So think very hard and clearly about your relationship. Will it withstand 24/7, small space? Any cracks that are in it now, try and repair them. Boats are never clean. If "cleanliness is next to godliness", if that is your motto don't buy a boat. It's like living in a house with the doors... all the doors and all the windows and the roof lights - everything open 24/7. Because you have dust, and anything that happens to be hanging around in the air, inside your boat immediately. And not only dirt and dust, you get unwanted visitors! We have been fighting a battle against cockroaches ever since we left the Mediterranean. Sometimes I win; sometimes I lose. You get ants, you get mosquitoes, you get all kinds of things. And worse than that, you can get rats as well! So be prepared for your lovely clean home to be invaded at all times, and for it not to be a lovely clean home. Unless you're one of these types that loves cleaning all day and all night! And going back to all of those things that you've got to be on the boat - in the mechanic, the engineer, the plumber, the electrician. Whilst you're doing all that work, your tools are going to be left everywhere. Liz absolutely hates this, she gets so wound up by it. Of course she forgets that I'm doing all the maintenance. Not quite all the maintenance! I do do the plumbing, but I put my stuff away when I finish. It's all over the bloody boat all the time, it drives me up the wall. Because you've only got this much space to live in. So yeah it's gonna be a quite a culture shock when you realize just how dirty a boat can get. Boats will change the way you look. Yep, you don't have much space on a boat. And your wardrobe will be compromised, so you will have to pare down all your fancy clothes to a very small, little space. It was heart-rendering when I had to reduce my enormous wardrobe in order to move onto this boat. But I had to think very carefully about functionality over beauty. So out went all the pretty dresses and the high heels, and I now pretty much wear what you're seeing, which is shorts and t-shirts, all the time. So my wardrobe, or my little tiny locker, is filled with those things. In a cold climate you're gonna have bigger things, bulkier things. And so you have even fewer of those because there is so little room on a boat to keep stuff. Don't forget that when you're on a boat you are rationing your water. Even if you have a water-maker pretty much every boat will always be keeping an eye on how much water you are using throughout the day and the week. So that of course means that washing clothes can really drain your water resources. But also washing yourself as well. So I think we'll be the first to admit we tend to wash... slightly... not as much as we should do. And that's because we have to keep an eye on water. Yeah, cruisers are notorious for being grubby. You wear the same thing for several days, and you don't wash for several days, but we get used to it! And finally just one quick word to the ladies out there: forget makeup. Makeup just falls off in the sun, and when you're sailing it falls off in the sea spray. And it's a pain in the butt. So go natural! You don't need make-up anyway darling... And finally... boats eat money. You can plan all you want, budget all you want, but you never ever have enough money. No, one of the big things that you have to think about is new parts because things, as we said earlier, go wrong all the time. And if the thing that goes wrong is an engine that's ten grand! Straight away! You've got to buy a new engine. Rigging - you have to re-rig every ten years, if you're insured they require it. And rigging something like Esper is really expensive. Sails, brand-new sails. Sails blow out. So these are really big expenses, and unless you've got huge savings somewhere, you've got to find a way, and be prepared to pay for these things. Yeah and these things happen when you least expect it. So forget about a monthly budget. We see a lot of questions from newbie sailors who say what's your monthly budget for yachting? Well we started doing a monthly budget, and it just went out the window. You can have a monthly budget for your day-to-day expenses, but the big expenses they really do come and bite you on the bum. Yeah, so what we tend to say is have a monthly income and work to that income, which is what we try to do. And finally, boats are not an investment. You never make money on boats, you really don't. When we bought Esper, 12 years ago, we paid slightly less than what she's insured for now, but in that time we've paid the same money again, and more on updates, parts, refitting everything. So she's insured for probably half of what we paid for her. They really don't make money. So those are five genuine, honest reasons as to why not to buy a boat. So if you are in the market for a buying a boat, I hope these will help you galvanize your thoughts, because these come from experience. This is from our 12 years, our own personal observations, and what other people have told us as well. So just take them on board, they are serious considerations. BUT, having said all that, Liz, why should you buy a boat? Oh, because when you've worked through all the bad times (and there are a lot of them) they absolutely, completely disappear when you're having the good times. Thanks for watching this followtheboat extra. If you have a comment please put it below, we're very interested to hear from you on this subject. If you have a boat, or you're thinking of buying a boat, what's your experience been? And if you liked it, please "like". And and if you really liked it, please "share". Peace and fair winds!

Obviously, his experience was vastly different than the average charter owner but, on the whole, Trey found the deal pretty positive. Maintenance other than cosmetic upkeep can be somewhat indifferent, especially given that the boats need only function on a limited range merry-go-round charter course meaning that owners taking over their boats after charter can expect to spend some time and money making the boats cruise-worthy. The cloud hanging over the entire issue is the continued viability of tropical charter operations in a world where Category 4 and 5 hurricanes are becoming an annual norm.  At some point, insurance rates and availability must suffer.

- How to Start an LLC for Boat & Yacht Charter Business (Step By Step) LLC for Boat Ownership Business: Kind: captions Language: en [Music] chartering is a term for hiring out a boat some owners Charter their boats on an occasional basis to help with running costs others make arrangements with a charter management company to arrange bare boat and crude yacht charters for them either way if you're thinking about chartering your boat you should be aware of the many applicable rules and regulations if you want to avoid getting yourself in deep water whatever the type of Charter Arrangement chartering can expose you to significant risk if you fail to plan and take sensible precautions with that in mind one option to consider is to place your boat in a limited liability company this video is a basic overview of how to set up an LLC before you make the decision to set up an LLC consult with an attorney or a tax professional to determine if this scenario is suitable for your situation the following discussion provides a general overview of the steps in setting up an LLC and the implications of having ownership of a boat in an LLC Step 1 choose a business name the first step in starting an LLC for your boat charter business is to choose a name your name should be unique easy to remember and easy to spell make sure to check with your State's business registration office to ensure that the name you've chosen is available Step 2 what state is the best to file your LLC some states offer llc's more financial advantages than others and that means you should carefully weigh your options before filing filing in your home state may be a good option for owners who have a home physical presence I.E work and or slip and conduct the vast majority or all of their business iity chartering in their home state if you meet these criteria your best option may be to file in your home state to save money annually because the LLC will not have to register as a foreign LLC if it conducts business in that state note that doing business generally requires an active business presence if not a physical office also an LLC that does business in its state of filing does not need to find and pay a registered agent to represent its interests within that state another popular choice for those forming an LLC continues to be Delaware Delaware has a solid reputation as one of the most business-friendly jurisdictions in the country notably Delaware does not tax out of state income which can mean an enormous tax savings for Delaware LLCs that do little or no business in the state itself in addition its initial filing fees and franchise taxes are quite low other favorable jurisdictions are Nevada and Wyoming step 3 choose a registered agent a registered agent is a person or business that receives legal and tax documents on behalf of your LLC the registered agent must be available during business hours to receive legal notices and should have a physical address in the state where the LLC is registered you may choose to be your own registered agent or use a professional registered agent service step 4 filed articles of organization after settling on a name you must prepare and file articles of organization with your State's LLC filing office while Most states use the term articles of organization to refer to the basic document required to create an LLC some states call it a certificate of formation or certificate of organization one disadvantage of forming an LLC instead of a partnership or a sole proprietorship is that you will have to pay a filing fee when you submit your articles of organization in Most states the fees are modest typically around 100 dollars a few other states cost more California for example charges an 800 annual tax on top of its filing fee you will likely have to list the name and address of a person usually one of the LLC members who will act as the llc's registered agent or agent for service of process the agent is the person designated to receive legal papers in any future lawsuit involving the LLC step 5 create an operating agreement an operating agreement is a legal document that outlines the ownership and operating procedures of your LLC while it's not required by law it's a good idea to have one in place to help prevent disputes and ensure everyone is on the same page your operating agreement should include the names and roles of all LLC members how profits and losses will be distributed how decisions will be made and any other important details about the operation of your business step 6 obtain insurance obtaining insurance is crucial for any business including a boat charter business you should consider obtaining liability insurance which covers damages and injuries cost to others while on board your boat you may also want to consider obtaining whole insurance which covers damages to Additionally you may want to consider obtaining business Interruption insurance which provides coverage in the event that your business is interrupted due to a covered loss step 7 obtain financing starting a boat charter business can require significant capital you may need to obtain financing to cover expenses such as purchasing a boat marketing and operating costs you can consider traditional financing options such as bank loans or alternative financing options such as crowdfunding or peer-to-peer Lending step 8 open a business bank account separating your personal and business finances is important for tax and liability purposes you should open a separate business bank account to keep track of your business income and expenses this will also help you establish business credit which can be useful when applying for financing in the future step 9 maintain compliance once your LLC is up and running it is important to maintain compliance with state and federal laws this includes filing annual reports and paying taxes on time you should also keep accurate records of your business transactions and financials it may be helpful to hire an accountant or bookkeeper to assist with these tasks what are the LLC benefits when selling a in California the purchase of a corporation or an LLC that owns a boat as its sole asset is not subject to sales or use tax however the step transaction Doctrine may be used to scrutinize transactions to determine if the LLC was set up solely to avoid tax to avoid this the LLC should have a legitimate business purpose and have been established for a significant amount of time additionally selling an entity rather than the boat itself can add complexity to the transaction as buyers may be unfamiliar with llc's or corporations and their Associated risks and liabilities how to transfer the boat ownership to LLC if the boat was not purchased by the LLC it needs to be transferred to the LLC and registered in its name however transferring the boat's title from the individual owner to the LLC May trigger an acceleration clause and a promissory note or preferred mortgage the lender may not consent to the transfer as it limits their recourse if the loan payments stop the owner should inform the lender of the transfer to avoid a default on the loan and the lender May accept a guarantee from the owner to protect against the risk of pursuing the LLC there may also be tax implications in the transfer and it is recommended to seek assistance from an attorney or tax professional in conclusion starting an LLC for a boat charter business involves several steps including choosing a business name filing articles of organization obtaining necessary licenses and permits obtaining Insurance obtaining financing and marketing your business it is important to maintain compliance with state and federal laws to ensure the success and longevity of your with proper planning and execution your boat charter business can provide a rewarding and profitable venture if you are confused or consider these steps to be difficult you can 1. Be placed into service in a U.S. trade or business, by a U.S.-based LLC.
2. Spend 50% or more of its actual usage (days or miles) in U.S. waters or territories.
SO if the boat is in charter in the BVI's, the boat cannot be bonus-depreciated, and you'd follow the longer ADS schedule instead.

- Nadine, not Naomi - The story of the Wolf of Wall Street Yacht: Kind: captions Language: en Today we will be talking about the Naoi, the 167 ft yacht owned by Jordan Belelfford, the former millionaire made famous for his financial frauds and more notably his memoirs in the movie The Wolf of Wall Street. Before we get into it, despite the name of the yacht being the Naomi in the movie as named after Jordan's second wife Naomi the Duchess of Bay Bridge, the actual name of the yacht is the Naen, which is her actual name. Naen, not the Naomi. Before it was the Naen, the ship sailed under a few other names as it had other owners before Jordan and before ending up at the bottom of the Mediterranean Sea. Despite people's strange affection for the rich suffering out on the water, RIP Ocean Gate, the yacht actually has an interesting history filled with fashion icons, murder, and other influential people, which is worth talking about before we get into its demise in 1996. The yacht was born under the name Matilda. It was built in 1961 in Holland and at the time was 130 ft long. The yacht was like none other in the 1960s with five stateaterooms, beautiful dining facilities, and many other amenities only suitable for the super rich. The name Matilda was short-lived as the original buyer backed out and after a year or so, the Chanel family bought it and renamed it the Coco Chanel. Coco Chanel is known more famously for being the founder of the Chanel brand, but always had an attraction to yaching. She was actually once quoted saying, "As soon as you set foot on a yacht, you belong to some man, not to yourself, and you die of boredon." So naturally, her solution was to buy her own yacht. The yacht sailed under the Coca-Cola Chanel name for 14 years until it was sold again in 1977. Melvin Powers, a Texas millionaire and real estate developer, bought the yacht and renamed it the Jan Pamela. Melvin Powers has his own unique story, one that deserves its own movie, or at the very least a Netflix documentary. A Texas Millionaire on its own is pretty boring, but a Texas millionaire who was acquitted of murdering his aunt's husband is interesting enough. What makes it even more interesting is that Melvin Powers had an incestuous relationship with his aunt back in 1962, well before he ever bought the yacht. Roll Tide. Before we get to all that, a little backstory on Melvin is needed. Melvin was a navy man. He worked many jobs and then ended up being convicted and sent to jail for pulling a con job in Pontiac, Michigan, which just so happens to be LA home state and not very far from where I grew up. He served his 90 days in jail and then while on probation decided to move to Houston. While there, his mother convinced him to reach out to her sister Candy Mosler who lived in the area with her husband Jack. Katie and Jack Moser were very well off as Jack had owned a number of financial companies. Jack Mosler found out about this weird affair that his wife was having with her nephew through his servants and after reading about it in his wife's diary. He avoided divorce due to not wanting any negative publicity as well as remembering the prenuptual agreement that he had signed, which means old dirty aunt Candy would get half of everything he had. Candi and Jack did eventually separate and after they did, Candy gave Melvin money to buy a mobile home business. During the murder trial, many employees of Jack Mosler said that Melvin Powers made several threats that he would kill Jack. Then in 1964, when Aunt Candy returned home, she found her husband's body wrapped in a blanket. Jack Moser had been bludgeoned and then stabbed 39 times. Both Candy and Melvin were on trial for the murder, and the trial took place two years after Jack was found dead. The New York Times described it as one of the most spectacular homicide trials ever. The jury eventually accepted the defense's case, voted for a quiddle, and Melvin and Aunt Candy walked free. No one else was ever charged for the murder of Jack Mosler. A fitting end to this story was after the trial was over and both Jack and Candy were acquitted, they were seen kissing and then driving off into the sunset in a gold Cadillac. Their ancestral relationship continued for a few years before they eventually separated. Aunt Candy later married a guy 19 younger than she was, but a year after they married, the poor guy mysteriously fell from a balcony of their mansion. He did live, but suffered brain damage and Candy divorced him shortly after. Aunt Candy eventually died of an overdose on her own medication in 1976. Melvin did attend his aunt's funeral, and in typical rich guy fashion who's attending the funeral of an ex-lover, he did show up with a younger, attractive blonde woman. But getting back to the yacht, a year after Aunt Candy's death, he purchased it and renamed it the Jan Pamela. Under his ownership, the now Jam Pamela was sent off to Miami to get some upgrades. Originally, they started by adding a 10- foot long fishing cockpit and then later on added another 27 feet to the yacht. They literally cut the ship in two pieces, welded in this section, and then increased its total length to its final dimension of 167 ft. This new addition made room for a helipad, two more staterooms, bringing the total up to seven, a salon, and even a jacuzzi with mirrored ceilings. In 1983, Melba did go bankrupt. The ship was sold and had a new name, the Edgewater. And for those curious, Melvin Powers died at the age of 68 in 2010. The name Edgewater didn't last long as in 1989 it was sold and bought by two people, Mark Elliot and his employer, Bernie Little. Both Mark and Bernie had a lifelong love for this yacht back when it was owned by the Chanel family. And Bernie actually loved it so much that when he got the chance to buy it, he bought it sight unseen. Birdie made his fortunes by owning a distribution business for Annheiser Bush. Just like the rest though, they chose to rename it. Now called the Big Eagle, and under new ownership, the yacht yet again went under the knife for some much needed upgrades. They pulled out all the windows, recchromeed them, repainted the entire yacht, and even pulled the engines and replaced them with two much larger CAT engines, which doubled the horsepower to 800. The yacht even had a massive glass panel hidden under a pair of sofas. When a button was pushed, the sofas were lifted up by hydraulics, revealing the glass, which gave you a view of the sea under the yacht. The Big Eagle was now a popular charter yacht. Some of the clients included Bert Reynolds and Open Winfrey. But guess who her favorite client was. Jordan Belelffort fell in love with the yacht during all of his charters. So much in fact, that he begged Bernie to sell it to him. Jordan still needed to secure financing, but Bernie agreed to hold a note on the yacht for Jordan in 1995, so long as Mark Elliot would stay on as skipper. Jordan eventually took ownership in 1996, changed the yacht's name to its final name of the Naen. Remember, not Naomi, Naen, kept Mark on as a skipper, and continued to use the yacht personally as well as chartered it out. He also chose to upgrade the Naen with some interior restyling, loads of mirrors, extending the upper deck, and even adding a crane that was capable of picking up and lowering a sea plane. It also held a lot more than just a sea plane, including a 30ft tender, two 18 ft dingies, four motorcycles, six jet skis, and a ton of professional diving gear. Mark Elliot later confirmed the crazy parties Jordan would have on board. But to him, Mark's job was only to protect the Naen. He was even quoted saying that Jordan was a brilliant and lovely person, but when he was in party mode, he was out of control. The fun was short-lived. Jordan and crew were making an 85 mile trip between Italy and Sardinia when a at the time 20 knot storm turned into a 70 knot storm that had waves that crested almost 35 ft. The Denine took on the storm quite well, holding its own for a few hours until the storm slowly started to chip away at the yacht. Among the first item lost to the storm was the 30-foot tender. After being beaten by the waves, the lights towing the boat finally gave way and the $100,000 toy was gone. The Nadine was slowly creeping through the storm at three or four knots when the massive wave smashed the for deck, breaking it apart like nothing, leaving a 3x4 ft hole open to be filled by the next wave and the waves after that. At that point, the Nadine was taking on water and being torn apart by the violet Mediterranean. About an hour after the four deck had been destroyed, one of the 18 ft jetboats that had been strapped to the deck came loose and smashed through the dining room windows. The Naiden's bow was now nearly underwater and water was rushing through the dining room into the middle of the yacht. Water made its way through the salon and into some of the stateaterooms. At that point, Mark Elliot knew the Nadine was severely crippled and used a satellite phone to contact the Italian Coast Guard. Mark then met with the passengers and crew, told them the situation, and slowly, one by one, they all went to their rooms to collect passports and anything of value that was small enough to fit into a bag. When the Italian Coast Guard got the call, they immediately sent out a rescue helicopter. They even sent out a large, slower, but dependable rescue boat with other larger ships on standby. 30 minutes after Mark made the call, the rescue helicopter showed up to attempt to airlift passengers out. The helicopter lowered down a rescue diver, but between the dark, the violent storm, and the rolling waves, the diver wasn't able to make contact. The diner himself actually was almost lost after crashing into the crane in other parts of the yacht. He was eventually lifted back up and the helicopter was forced to take off. Several hours had passed and no one had been rescued yet. The storm got even worse and anything that was on the deck of the Naen was now lost. The larger ships that were on standby were also having troubles of their own trying to launch a rescue ship to head to the Naen. Also, at one point in the night, a merchant ship that was nearby came to try to help the Naen, but almost accidentally ran into it, coming as close as 15 feet from the yacht. Mark was able to maneuver the Naen out of the way and avoided further disaster. There were several attempts throughout the night to send helicopters to rescue, but they all had the same issues as the first one. High seas, high winds, and low visibility made it almost impossible to get to it. The NAM was sinking more and more into the water, and at this point, some crew members were set to retrieve the life rafts to get ready to be used. But before they could get to one of the three life rafts, one of the trigger lights to a raft had gone off, inflating the raft and sending it over the side. A strong gust of wind had come in and tore the life raft away from the yacht, and it was never seen again. Now, around 3:00 a.m., the commander of the Coast Guard made contact with Mark and put together a plan. They decided that they needed to clear away the helipad in order for a rescue to be possible, which meant that the helicopter that was strapped to the helellipad would need to be lost. Mark was reluctant to do so, but him and the crew made efforts to release the straps that held the helicopter to the pad, and then he steered the naen in a direction that would allow the storm to sweep the helicopter off. The storm eventually swept the helicopter off the pad, and the helicopter was lost at sea. Now, around 5:00 a.m., the rescue helicopters from the Coast Guard were set back out, and the rescue was finally able to take place. All the people on the Denine went in separate groups, starting with the female passengers, then the female crew, then the male guests, and lastly, some of the male crew members. Mark and a couple of crew members stayed on board the NAD, still optimistic that they could save her. Mark and the remaining crew members loaded into the life raft grabbed all the passports, some logs, and some of the personal belongings of the passengers as they were not allowed to take them with them when the Coast Guard picked them up. Mark's last official active duty aboard the Naen and at the request of the Coast Guard was to go into the engine room and shut everything down. He did so, boarded the lifeboat, and was able to get out and away with the remaining crew members. 10 minutes after they were all cleared of the Naen, she was gone. Mark was congratulated for what he was able to do with the Naen and getting every single person off of the ship alive and uninjured. He was even offered command of Bernie Little's next large charter yacht. Mark has always kept his feelings about the sinking of the Naen a secret, but noted that his biggest regret was leaving behind his banjo. The insurance was quick to pay out for the loss of the Naen considering the size and severity of the storm. The only thing that remained from the Naen that was recovered without a scratch was the 30-foot tender that broke free early on. Now the Naen rests 1,000 m below the surface of the Mediterranean Sea just 20 m east of the coast of Sardinia. She was in operation for 35 years, saw numerous owners, transformations, and was likely host to some wild stories. From the Matilda to the Coco Chanel to Jan Pamela to Edgewater to Big Eagle and finally the Naen, she lived a wild life. I just hope all the new owners followed the rules of renaming a boat, which I'll cover in another video. This was the Naen. Thank you for watching. Thank you for listening.
